topics.title: Review of Basic Calculus Concepts - Stationary Points
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT723 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Review of Basic Calculus Concepts - Stationary Points

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stationary Point (KTU Definition):**
> A point $x_0$ in the domain of a real-valued function $f: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$ is called a **stationary point** if the gradient of the function vanishes at that point, i.e., $\nabla f(x_0) = \mathbf{0}$. For a single-variable function $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$, a point $x_0$ is stationary if $f'(x_0) = 0$.

In the context of **optimization techniques**, identifying stationary points is the *first and most fundamental step* in locating candidate solutions for unconstrained optimization problems. The KTU 2024 scheme places this concept in Module 1 because every numerical method (Gradient Descent, Newton's Method, Lagrange Multipliers) and every heuristic (PSO, GA evaluation) implicitly relies on the mathematical structure of stationary points.

### Types of Stationary Points

A stationary point $x_0$ of $f(x)$ can be classified as one of three types:

1. **Local Minimum** — the function attains its lowest value in a neighborhood of $x_0$.
2. **Local Maximum** — the function attains its highest value in a neighborhood of $x_0$.
3. **Saddle Point (Inflection Point)** — the function neither attains a maximum nor a minimum; the curvature changes sign.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Stationary"?**
> The term *stationary* comes from physics: at such a point, the instantaneous rate of change of the function is **zero**, meaning the function momentarily "stands still" (stationary = standing still). In mechanics, this corresponds to equilibrium positions of a system.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are **hiking up and down a mountain range** in Kerala's Western Ghats. As you walk along the trail, your altitude $h$ changes with horizontal position $x$:

- At a **mountain peak**, you stop rising and start descending — the slope momentarily becomes zero. This is a **local maximum** (stationary point).
- At the **bottom of a valley**, you stop descending and start rising — slope is again zero. This is a **local minimum** (stationary point).
- At a **mountain pass (saddle)**, you might be descending in one direction and ascending in another. The slope is zero in both, but it's neither a peak nor a valley — a **saddle point**.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Connection:** In structural engineering, a beam's deflection curve has stationary points at points of maximum bending and at supports. In economics, profit functions have stationary points at break-even and optimum-output levels. In machine learning, the loss function's stationary points are candidates for the global minimum that every optimizer seeks.

## 1.3 Critical vs. Stationary Points

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction (Frequently Tested in KTU):**
> - **Stationary point** of $f$: a point where $f'(x) = 0$ (the derivative exists and equals zero).
> - **Critical point** of $f$: a point where either $f'(x) = 0$ **OR** $f'(x)$ does not exist.
>
> Therefore, every stationary point is a critical point, but the converse is not always true. For example, $x = 0$ is a critical point of $f(x) = x^{1/3}$ (since $f'$ is undefined there), but it is **not** a stationary point.

## 1.4 The Bigger Picture in Optimization

The KTU 2024 syllabus frames this topic as the **review foundation** for all subsequent modules. The logical flow is:

| Stage | Mathematical Tool | Module Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Identify candidate points | Stationary points ($f'(x)=0$) | Module 1 (Current) |
| Classify them | First/Second Derivative Test | Module 1 (Current) |
| Constrained cases | Lagrange Multipliers | Module 2 |
| Numerical search | Gradient Descent, Newton's Method | Module 3 / 4 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Necessary Condition for a Stationary Point (Single Variable)

**Theorem (Fermat's Theorem on Stationary Points):**
If $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is differentiable at $x_0$ and $x_0$ is a local extremum (max or min), then $f'(x_0) = 0$.

> [!NOTE]
> The converse is **not** true: $f'(x_0) = 0$ does not guarantee an extremum (counter-example: $f(x) = x^3$ at $x_0 = 0$, which is an inflection point).

## 2.2 First Derivative Test (Sign-Change Method)

This is the most elementary classification method. The idea is to inspect the sign of $f'(x)$ on the immediate left and right of $x_0$:

- If $f'(x) < 0$ for $x < x_0$ and $f'(x) > 0$ for $x > x_0$ → $x_0$ is a **Local Minimum** (function was decreasing, now increasing).
- If $f'(x) > 0$ for $x < x_0$ and $f'(x) < 0$ for $x > x_0$ → $x_0$ is a **Local Maximum**.
- If $f'(x)$ does not change sign at $x_0$ → $x_0$ is a **Saddle / Inflection Point**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this works:** A negative derivative means the function is decreasing, a positive one means increasing. A sign change from negative to positive "bottoms out" at the zero — this is a minimum. The opposite gives a maximum.

## 2.3 Second Derivative Test (Concavity Method)

This is the most widely used method in KTU board examinations because it requires only pointwise evaluation:

1. Solve $f'(x) = 0$ to get candidate stationary points $x_0$.
2. Compute the second derivative $f''(x)$.
3. Evaluate $f''(x_0)$:
   - If $f''(x_0) > 0$ → $x_0$ is a **Local Minimum** (function is convex, curve bends upward).
   - If $f''(x_0) < 0$ → $x_0$ is a **Local Maximum** (function is concave, curve bends downward).
   - If $f''(x_0) = 0$ → **Test is Inconclusive**; use the first derivative test or higher-order test.

## 2.4 Higher-Order Derivative Test (When $f''(x_0) = 0$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Theorem — Higher-Order Test:**
> If $f'(x_0) = f''(x_0) = \cdots = f^{(n-1)}(x_0) = 0$ and $f^{(n)}(x_0) \neq 0$, then:
> - If $n$ is **even** and $f^{(n)}(x_0) > 0$ → **Local Minimum**.
> - If $n$ is **even** and $f^{(n)}(x_0) < 0$ → **Local Maximum**.
> - If $n$ is **odd** → **Point of Inflection** (neither max nor min).

## 2.5 Extension to Multivariable Functions

For $f: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$, a stationary point $\mathbf{x}^*$ satisfies:

$$
\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} \\ \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} \\ \vdots \\ \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_n} \end{bmatrix}_{\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{x}^*} = \mathbf{0}
$$

The **Hessian matrix** at $\mathbf{x}^*$ is:

$$
H(\mathbf{x}^*) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1^2} & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1 \partial x_2} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1 \partial x_n} \\ \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2 \partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2^2} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2 \partial x_n} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_n \partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_n \partial x_2} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_n^2} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Multivariable Classification Rule:**
- If $H$ is **positive definite** at $\mathbf{x}^*$ → **Local Minimum**.
- If $H$ is **negative definite** at $\mathbf{x}^*$ → **Local Maximum**.
- If $H$ is **indefinite** → **Saddle Point**.
- If $H$ is **singular** (determinant = 0) → **Test Inconclusive**.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Single Variable $\mathbb{R}$ | Multivariable $\mathbb{R}^n$ |
|---|---|---|
| Stationary Condition | $f'(x_0) = 0$ | $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$ |
| First Derivative Test | Sign of $f'$ changes across $x_0$ | N/A (use Hessian) |
| Second Derivative Test | $f''(x_0) > 0 \Rightarrow$ Min; $f''(x_0) < 0 \Rightarrow$ Max | $H$ positive $\Rightarrow$ Min; $H$ negative $\Rightarrow$ Max |
| Higher-Order Test | First nonzero derivative is of even order $\Rightarrow$ Extremum | N/A |
| Saddle Condition | Odd-order first nonzero derivative at $x_0$ | $H$ indefinite |
| Inconclusive Case | $f''(x_0) = 0$ | $\det(H) = 0$ |
| Necessary (Fermat) | $f'(x_0) = 0$ for interior extremum | $\nabla f = \mathbf{0}$ for interior extremum |
| Sufficient | $f''(x_0) \neq 0$ with correct sign | $H$ definite with correct sign |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** In control systems, stationary points of the cost functional $J = \int L \, dt$ give the Euler-Lagrange equations. In structural design, minimizing weight subject to stress gives a stationary condition. In machine learning, every neural-network training algorithm attempts to find stationary points of the loss surface.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation — Higher-Order Test (Taylor's Theorem Approach)

We want to justify **why** the first non-vanishing derivative of even order with positive sign gives a local minimum.

**Step 1: Start with the Taylor expansion of $f$ around $x_0$ up to order $n$:**

By Taylor's theorem, for $h$ small (let $x = x_0 + h$):

$$
f(x_0 + h) = f(x_0) + h f'(x_0) + \frac{h^2}{2!} f''(x_0) + \frac{h^3}{3!} f'''(x_0) + \cdots + \frac{h^n}{n!} f^{(n)}(x_0) + o(h^n)
$$

**Step 2: Apply the stationary condition:**

Since $x_0$ is a stationary point, $f'(x_0) = 0$. Also, by the higher-order test hypothesis, $f''(x_0) = f'''(x_0) = \cdots = f^{(n-1)}(x_0) = 0$. Therefore all intermediate terms vanish:

$$
f(x_0 + h) - f(x_0) = \frac{h^n}{n!} f^{(n)}(x_0) + o(h^n)
$$

**Step 3: Asymptotic analysis:**

For sufficiently small $h$, the higher-order remainder $o(h^n)$ is negligible compared to the leading term $\frac{h^n}{n!} f^{(n)}(x_0)$. So the sign of $f(x_0 + h) - f(x_0)$ is governed by the leading term:

$$
\text{sign}\bigl(f(x_0 + h) - f(x_0)\bigr) \;\approx\; \text{sign}\!\left(\frac{h^n}{n!} f^{(n)}(x_0)\right) = \text{sign}\!\left(h^n \cdot f^{(n)}(x_0)\right)
$$

since $n! > 0$ always.

**Step 4: Case analysis:**

- If $n$ is **even**: $h^n > 0$ for all $h \neq 0$ (both positive and negative $h$ give positive $h^n$).
  - If $f^{(n)}(x_0) > 0$: then $f(x_0 + h) - f(x_0) > 0$ for all small $h \neq 0$ → $x_0$ is a **Local Minimum**.
  - If $f^{(n)}(x_0) < 0$: then $f(x_0 + h) - f(x_0) < 0$ for all small $h \neq 0$ → $x_0$ is a **Local Maximum**.
- If $n$ is **odd**: $h^n$ changes sign with $h$, so the leading term changes sign with $h$.
  - For $h > 0$: sign = sign of $f^{(n)}(x_0)$.
  - For $h < 0$: sign = opposite of sign of $f^{(n)}(x_0)$.
  - Therefore, $f$ lies above $f(x_0)$ on one side and below on the other → **Point of Inflection**.

**Step 5: Conclusion (the theorem statement):**

$$
\boxed{\;
\begin{aligned}
&\text{First non-zero derivative is } f^{(n)}(x_0). \\
&\bullet \; n \text{ even}, \; f^{(n)}(x_0) > 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Local Minimum} \\
&\bullet \; n \text{ even}, \; f^{(n)}(x_0) < 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Local Maximum} \\
&\bullet \; n \text{ odd} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Point of Inflection (Saddle)}
\end{aligned}\;}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Example — Full Single-Variable Classification

**Problem:** Find and classify all stationary points of $f(x) = x^4 - 4x^3 + 4x^2 + 2$.

**Step 1: Compute the first derivative (using power rule):**

$$
f'(x) = 4x^3 - 12x^2 + 8x
$$

**Step 2: Factor and solve $f'(x) = 0$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
4x^3 - 12x^2 + 8x &= 0 \\
4x(x^2 - 3x + 2) &= 0 \\
4x(x - 1)(x - 2) &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore the **stationary points** are $x = 0$, $x = 1$, $x = 2$.

**Step 3: Compute the second derivative:**

$$
f''(x) = 12x^2 - 24x + 8
$$

**Step 4: Evaluate $f''(x)$ at each stationary point:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f''(0) &= 12(0)^2 - 24(0) + 8 = 8 > 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Local Minimum at } x = 0. \\
f''(1) &= 12(1)^2 - 24(1) + 8 = 12 - 24 + 8 = -4 < 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Local Maximum at } x = 1. \\
f''(2) &= 12(2)^2 - 24(2) + 8 = 48 - 48 + 8 = 8 > 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Local Minimum at } x = 2.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute the function values (for completeness):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(0) &= 0 - 0 + 0 + 2 = 2 \\
f(1) &= 1 - 4 + 4 + 2 = 3 \\
f(2) &= 16 - 32 + 16 + 2 = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Summary table:**

| Point $x_0$ | $f''(x_0)$ | Classification | Function Value $f(x_0)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $0$ | $8 > 0$ | Local Minimum | $2$ |
| $1$ | $-4 < 0$ | Local Maximum | $3$ |
| $2$ | $8 > 0$ | Local Minimum | $2$ |

## 3.3 Worked Example — Multivariable Case

**Problem:** Find and classify the stationary points of $f(x, y) = x^3 + y^3 - 3xy$.

**Step 1: Compute the partial derivatives:**

$$
\frac{\partial f}{\partial x} = 3x^2 - 3y, \qquad \frac{\partial f}{\partial y} = 3y^2 - 3x
$$

**Step 2: Set the gradient to zero:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
3x^2 - 3y &= 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; y = x^2 \\
3y^2 - 3x &= 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; x = y^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Solve the system.**

Substitute $y = x^2$ into $x = y^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= (x^2)^2 = x^4 \\
x^4 - x &= 0 \\
x(x^3 - 1) &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Solutions: $x = 0$ or $x^3 = 1 \Rightarrow x = 1$.

Corresponding $y$ values: $y = x^2$, so $y = 0$ or $y = 1$.

**Stationary points:** $(0, 0)$ and $(1, 1)$.

**Step 4: Compute the Hessian matrix components:**

$$
\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x^2} = 6x, \quad \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial y^2} = 6y, \quad \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x \partial y} = \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial y \partial x} = -3
$$

So:

$$
H(x, y) = \begin{bmatrix} 6x & -3 \\ -3 & 6y \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5: Evaluate Hessian at each stationary point and check definiteness using the leading principal minors test.**

**At $(0, 0)$:**

$$
H(0, 0) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -3 \\ -3 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Leading minors: $D_1 = 0$ (not $> 0$), $D_2 = (0)(0) - (-3)^2 = -9 < 0$. Since the determinant is negative, $H$ is **indefinite** → $(0, 0)$ is a **Saddle Point**.

**At $(1, 1)$:**

$$
H(1, 1) = \begin{bmatrix} 6 & -3 \\ -3 & 6 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Leading minors: $D_1 = 6 > 0$, $D_2 = (6)(6) - (-3)^2 = 36 - 9 = 27 > 0$. Both positive → $H$ is **positive definite** → $(1, 1)$ is a **Local Minimum**.

Function value: $f(1, 1) = 1 + 1 - 3 = -1$.

**Step 6: Summary:**

| Point | Hessian Type | Classification | $f$ value |
|---|---|---|---|
| $(0, 0)$ | Indefinite ($\det = -9 < 0$) | Saddle Point | $0$ |
| $(1, 1)$ | Positive Definite ($D_1, D_2 > 0$) | Local Minimum | $-1$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Definiteness Shortcut:** For a $2 \times 2$ symmetric matrix $\begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ b & c \end{bmatrix}$: positive definite iff $a > 0$ and $ac - b^2 > 0$; negative definite iff $a < 0$ and $ac - b^2 > 0$; indefinite iff $ac - b^2 < 0$. The student must memorize this for the KTU exam.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flowchart: Stationary Point Classification Algorithm

This flowchart maps the decision logic a student must follow in the KTU examination when classifying a stationary point.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Given f of x"] --> B["Step 1 Compute f prime of x"]
    B --> C["Step 2 Solve f prime of x equals 0"]
    C --> D{"Step 3 Are real roots found?"}
    D -- "No real roots" --> E["No stationary points exist in reals"]
    D -- "Yes" --> F["Candidate points x not equal to empty set"]
    F --> G["Step 4 Compute f double prime of x"]
    G --> H{"Step 5 Evaluate f double prime at each x not equal to empty set"}
    H -- "f double prime greater than 0" --> I["Local MINIMUM at x not equal to empty set"]
    H -- "f double prime less than 0" --> J["Local MAXIMUM at x not equal to empty set"]
    H -- "f double prime equals 0" --> K["Test INCONCLUSIVE"]
    K --> L["Apply First Derivative Test or Higher Order Test"]
    L --> M["Final Classification Obtained"]
    I --> N["Report f of x not equal to empty set value"]
    J --> N
    M --> N
    E --> O["END"]
    N --> O
```

## 4.2 Concept Map: Types of Stationary Points

This block diagram organizes the taxonomy of stationary points as required by the KTU 2024 syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["STATIONARY POINT x not equal to empty set with f prime equals 0"] --> Q["Classification via Higher Order Test"]
    Q --> R["Local MINIMUM"]
    Q --> S["Local MAXIMUM"]
    Q --> T["SADDLE or INFLECTION POINT"]
    R --> R1["n even and f super n greater than 0"]
    R --> R2["f double prime greater than 0 in second derivative test"]
    S --> S1["n even and f super n less than 0"]
    S --> S2["f double prime less than 0 in second derivative test"]
    T --> T1["n odd in higher order test"]
    T --> T2["Hessian indefinite for multivariable"]
    T --> T3["f double prime equals 0 and no sign change of f prime"]
    P --> U["Multivariable Extension"]
    U --> V["Gradient equals 0 vector"]
    V --> W["Hessian H check definiteness"]
    W --> W1["H positive definite equals Local Min"]
    W --> W2["H negative definite equals Local Max"]
    W --> W3["H indefinite equals Saddle"]
    W --> W4["H singular equals Inconclusive"]
```

## 4.3 Comparative Schematic: Single Variable vs. Multivariable Procedure

This diagram contrasts the algorithmic steps for the two cases to make the parallel structure clear.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SUB1["SINGLE VARIABLE f of x"]
        A1["Compute f prime of x"] --> A2["Solve f prime of x equals 0"]
        A2 --> A3{"Second Derivative Test"}
        A3 -- "f double prime greater than 0" --> A4["Local Min"]
        A3 -- "f double prime less than 0" --> A5["Local Max"]
        A3 -- "f double prime equals 0" --> A6["Higher Order Test"]
    end
    subgraph SUB2["MULTIVARIABLE f of x vector"]
        B1["Compute gradient nabla f"] --> B2["Solve nabla f equals 0 vector"]
        B2 --> B3{"Hessian Definiteness Test"}
        B3 -- "H positive definite" --> B4["Local Min"]
        B3 -- "H negative definite" --> B5["Local Max"]
        B3 -- "H indefinite" --> B6["Saddle Point"]
        B3 -- "H singular" --> B7["Inconclusive"]
    end
    A1 -. "Analogous step" .-> B1
    A2 -. "Analogous step" .-> B2
    A3 -. "Analogous role" .-> B3
    A4 -. "Same conclusion" .-> B4
    A5 -. "Same conclusion" .-> B5
    A6 -. "Similar to" .-> B7
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**Define a stationary point of a function $f(x)$. Differentiate between a stationary point and a critical point with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

A point $x_0$ is called a **stationary point** of $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ if $f$ is differentiable at $x_0$ and $f'(x_0) = 0$. Geometrically, the tangent to the curve at $x_0$ is parallel to the $x$-axis.

A **critical point** is a point in the domain where $f'(x_0) = 0$ **or** $f'(x_0)$ does not exist. Hence every stationary point is a critical point, but the converse is not always true.

**Example:**
- $f(x) = x^2$: stationary point at $x_0 = 0$ (since $f'(0) = 0$). It is also critical.
- $f(x) = x^{1/3}$: $x_0 = 0$ is critical (since $f'(0)$ is undefined) but **not** a stationary point (since the derivative is not zero, it doesn't exist).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]

**State and explain the second derivative test for classifying stationary points. What is the inconclusive case?**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** Let $f$ be twice differentiable at $x_0$ and let $f'(x_0) = 0$. Then:
- If $f''(x_0) > 0$, then $x_0$ is a **local minimum**.
- If $f''(x_0) < 0$, then $x_0$ is a **local maximum**.

**Reason:** A positive $f''(x_0)$ means the function is locally convex (curving upward), so the function increases on both sides of $x_0$ — a minimum. A negative $f''(x_0)$ means the function is locally concave (curving downward) — a maximum.

**Inconclusive Case:** When $f''(x_0) = 0$, the test gives no information. For example, $f(x) = x^3$ at $x_0 = 0$ has $f''(0) = 0$ but the point is an inflection, not an extremum. In this case, we resort to the **first derivative test** or the **higher-order derivative test**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Find all stationary points of $f(x) = x^3 - 6x^2 + 9x + 1$ and classify them using the second derivative test.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Apply the higher-order derivative test to classify the stationary points of $g(x) = x^4 - 2x^2$ and verify your results geometrically.

---

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Step 1: First derivative**

$$
f'(x) = 3x^2 - 12x + 9
$$

**Step 2: Solve $f'(x) = 0$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
3x^2 - 12x + 9 &= 0 \\
x^2 - 4x + 3 &= 0 \\
(x - 1)(x - 3) &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

So the stationary points are $x_0 = 1$ and $x_0 = 3$.

**Step 3: Second derivative**

$$
f''(x) = 6x - 12
$$

**Step 4: Classify each stationary point**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f''(1) &= 6(1) - 12 = -6 < 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Local Maximum at } x_0 = 1. \\
f''(3) &= 6(3) - 12 = 6 > 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Local Minimum at } x_0 = 3.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Function values**

$$
f(1) = 1 - 6 + 9 + 1 = 5, \qquad f(3) = 27 - 54 + 27 + 1 = 1
$$

**Conclusion:** Local maximum at $(1, 5)$ and local minimum at $(3, 1)$.

---

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1: First derivative**

$$
g'(x) = 4x^3 - 4x = 4x(x^2 - 1) = 4x(x - 1)(x + 1)
$$

**Step 2: Stationary points**

$g'(x) = 0 \Rightarrow x = 0, \; x = 1, \; x = -1$.

**Step 3: Second derivative**

$$
g''(x) = 12x^2 - 4
$$

**Step 4: Test at $x = 1$ and $x = -1$**

$$
g''(1) = 12 - 4 = 8 > 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Local Minimum at } x_0 = 1. \\
g''(-1) = 12 - 4 = 8 > 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Local Minimum at } x_0 = -1.
$$

**Step 5: Test at $x = 0$ — inconclusive with second derivative**

$$
g''(0) = -4 < 0
$$

**Wait — correction:** $g''(0) = 12(0)^2 - 4 = -4 < 0$, so this gives a **Local Maximum** directly. No need for the higher-order test here.

**Step 6: For pedagogical demonstration, suppose the question demanded a higher-order test at $x = 0$.** Since $g''(0) = -4 \neq 0$, the second derivative test already suffices. The point $x_0 = 0$ is a local maximum.

**Step 7: Function values**

$$
g(-1) = 1 - 2 = -1, \quad g(0) = 0, \quad g(1) = 1 - 2 = -1
$$

**Geometric verification:** The graph of $g(x) = x^4 - 2x^2$ is a "W-shaped" quartic. It has two equal minima at $(\pm 1, -1)$ and a central local maximum at $(0, 0)$. This matches our classification perfectly.

**Conclusion:**
- Local minima at $x = \pm 1$ with $g = -1$.
- Local maximum at $x = 0$ with $g = 0$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023] — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

**(a) [7 Marks]** Find and classify the stationary points of the multivariable function $f(x, y) = x^2 + y^2 - 4x + 6y + 13$ using the Hessian test.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the function $h(x) = x^5 - 5x^4$, find all stationary points and classify each using the higher-order derivative test. State explicitly the order of the first non-vanishing derivative at each point.

---

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Step 1: Compute first partial derivatives**

$$
\frac{\partial f}{\partial x} = 2x - 4, \qquad \frac{\partial f}{\partial y} = 2y + 6
$$

**Step 2: Solve the gradient = 0**

$$
\begin{aligned}
2x - 4 &= 0 \;\Rightarrow\; x = 2 \\
2y + 6 &= 0 \;\Rightarrow\; y = -3
\end{aligned}
$$

The unique stationary point is $(2, -3)$.

**Step 3: Compute the Hessian**

$$
\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x^2} = 2, \quad \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial y^2} = 2, \quad \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x \partial y} = 0
$$

$$
H = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4: Definiteness test**

- $D_1 = 2 > 0$
- $D_2 = (2)(2) - (0)^2 = 4 > 0$

Since both leading principal minors are positive, $H$ is **positive definite**.

**Step 5: Classification**

The point $(2, -3)$ is a **strict local minimum**.

**Step 6: Function value**

$$
f(2, -3) = 4 + 9 - 8 - 18 + 13 = 0
$$

In fact, the function can be rewritten as $f(x, y) = (x-2)^2 + (y+3)^2$, so the point $(2, -3)$ is the **global minimum** with $f = 0$.

---

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1: First derivative**

$$
h'(x) = 5x^4 - 20x^3 = 5x^3(x - 4)
$$

**Step 2: Stationary points**

$$
5x^3(x - 4) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; x = 0 \text{ (multiplicity 3)} \;\text{or}\; x = 4
$$

**Step 3: Classify $x = 4$ using the second derivative test**

$$
h''(x) = 20x^3 - 60x^2
$$

$$
h''(4) = 20(64) - 60(16) = 1280 - 960 = 320 > 0
$$

So $x = 4$ is a **Local Minimum**.

**Step 4: Classify $x = 0$ — second derivative test is inconclusive**

$$
h''(0) = 0
$$

We need higher-order derivatives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h'''(x) &= 60x^2 - 120x \\
h^{(4)}(x) &= 120x - 120 \\
h^{(5)}(x) &= 120
\end{aligned}
$$

At $x = 0$:
- $h'(0) = 0$
- $h''(0) = 0$
- $h'''(0) = 0$
- $h^{(4)}(0) = -120$
- $h^{(5)}(0) = 120$

The **first non-vanishing derivative** is $h^{(4)}(0) = -120$, which is of **even order** $n = 4$ and is **negative**.

**Step 5: Apply the higher-order test rule**

$n$ even and $f^{(n)} < 0 \Rightarrow$ **Local Maximum**.

So $x = 0$ is a **Local Maximum**.

**Step 6: Function values**

$$
h(0) = 0, \qquad h(4) = 1024 - 1280 = -256
$$

**Summary:**

| Point $x_0$ | First Nonzero Derivative | Order | Sign | Classification | $h(x_0)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $0$ | $h^{(4)}(0) = -120$ | $4$ (even) | Negative | Local Maximum | $0$ |
| $4$ | $h''(4) = 320$ | $2$ (even) | Positive | Local Minimum | $-256$ |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes (Read Carefully!)**
> 1. **Forgetting to verify $f'(x_0) = 0$ before applying the second derivative test.** A positive $f''(x_0)$ at a non-stationary point means nothing about extrema.
> 2. **Confusing "critical point" and "stationary point" in the answer.** The KTU examiner specifically tests this distinction — losing 1 mark for imprecise terminology.
> 3. **Reporting only the $x$-coordinate of the stationary point.** Always report the **full coordinate** $(x_0, f(x_0))$ for a complete answer.
> 4. **Stating "maxima/minima" without specifying "local/global".** In KTU valuation, this loses 1 mark. Always say **"local minimum"** or **"strict local minimum"** etc.
> 5. **In Hessian questions, forgetting the leading principal minors test.** Simply saying "the Hessian is positive definite" without showing the $D_1, D_2 > 0$ computation costs 2 marks.
> 6. **Mixing up "point of inflection" and "saddle point"** — in single-variable calculus, the correct term is "inflection point"; "saddle point" is reserved for multivariable functions. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this swap.
> 7. **Skipping the higher-order test even when $f''(x_0) = 0$ and stopping at "inconclusive".** Full marks require the student to complete the classification using the higher-order test.
> 8. **Arithmetic errors in solving $f'(x) = 0$.** Always factor and double-check by substitution back into $f'$. A sign error costs the entire 7-mark sub-question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1 Stationary Points**

- **Definition:** Stationary point of $f$ is a point where $f'(x) = 0$ (single variable) or $\nabla f = \mathbf{0}$ (multivariable). The derivative must **exist** at that point.
- **Critical vs. Stationary:** Critical = $f'(x) = 0$ or $f'(x)$ does not exist. Stationary = $f'(x) = 0$ only. Stationary $\subseteq$ Critical.
- **Fermat's Theorem:** Interior local extrema of differentiable functions must be stationary points. (Converse is false: $f(x) = x^3$ at $0$ is stationary but not an extremum.)
- **First Derivative Test:** Observe sign change of $f'(x)$ across $x_0$: $(- \to +) \Rightarrow$ min; $(+ \to -) \Rightarrow$ max; no sign change $\Rightarrow$ inflection.
- **Second Derivative Test:** $f''(x_0) > 0 \Rightarrow$ local min; $f''(x_0) < 0 \Rightarrow$ local max; $f''(x_0) = 0 \Rightarrow$ **INCONCLUSIVE** — do not stop here!
- **Higher-Order Test:** First non-zero derivative $f^{(n)}(x_0)$: if $n$ even, classification by sign; if $n$ odd, inflection point.
- **Multivariable Procedure:** Set $\nabla f = \mathbf{0}$, then classify using Hessian leading principal minors. $D_1, D_2, \ldots, D_n > 0 \Rightarrow$ positive definite $\Rightarrow$ local min; alternating signs with $D_1 < 0 \Rightarrow$ negative definite $\Rightarrow$ local max.
- **Singular Hessian:** If $\det(H) = 0$, the test is inconclusive — fall back to direct analysis or Taylor expansion.
- **Saddle Point Identifier:** A stationary point that is neither a local max nor a local min. In single variable, it is an **inflection point** with horizontal tangent.
- **Always report** the **coordinate** $(x_0, f(x_0))$ and the **classification** (local/global min/max, or saddle/inflection) for full KTU marks.
- **Engineering relevance:** Loss functions in ML, cost functionals in optimal control, deflection curves in mechanics, profit functions in economics — all use stationary-point analysis as the foundation.
- **Key formula to memorize:**

$$
\boxed{\; \nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}, \quad H(\mathbf{x}^*) \text{ definite} \iff \text{strict local extremum} \;}
$$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Functions of Single and Two Variables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT723 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Functions of Single and Two Variables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Function?

In the language of **Optimization Techniques**, a *function* is a precise mathematical rule that maps every admissible input (or collection of inputs) to exactly one output. The output is a single numerical value (a real number), and the set of admissible inputs is called the **domain**, while the set of resulting outputs is called the **range**.

A function is formally denoted as:

$$f: D \subseteq \mathbb{R}^{n} \longrightarrow \mathbb{R}$$

where $D$ is the domain and $n$ denotes the number of independent input variables.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 1 of OEECT723, the focus is restricted to **single variable ($n=1$)** and **two variable ($n=2$)** functions, as these form the foundation for understanding *unconstrained optimization*, *stationary points*, and *Hessian-based tests*.

### 1.2 Classification of Functions

#### (a) Function of a Single Variable ($n = 1$)
A function $f: D \subseteq \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ takes a single real number $x$ as input and produces a single real number $f(x)$ as output.

**Example:** $f(x) = x^{2} + 3x - 5$, defined for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$.

#### (b) Function of Two Variables ($n = 2$)
A function $f: D \subseteq \mathbb{R}^{2} \to \mathbb{R}$ takes an *ordered pair* $(x, y)$ as input and produces a single real number $f(x, y)$ as output.

**Example:** $f(x, y) = x^{2} + y^{2} - 4x + 2y + 7$, defined on $\mathbb{R}^{2}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition):**
> - A **single-variable function** is like the reading on a *single-sensor digital thermometer* — only one input (temperature) drives the output (reading).
> - A **two-variable function** is like a *hiking map's elevation contours* — the height (output) depends on two inputs: your **east-west position** ($x$) and **north-south position** ($y$).
>
> In optimization, we essentially ask: *"At which point is the thermometer reading the lowest/highest?"* or *"Where on the map is the peak (or valley) located?"*

### 1.3 Essential Terminology (KTU Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Independent Variable** | An input quantity that is freely chosen by the decision-maker (e.g., $x$ or $y$). |
| **Dependent Variable** | The output of the function; its value *depends* on the independent variables. |
| **Domain $D$** | The set of all permissible input values (where the function is mathematically valid). |
| **Range $R$** | The set of all possible output values the function can produce. |
| **Level Set / Contour** | For $f(x,y) = c$, the locus of points in $\mathbb{R}^{2}$ producing the same output $c$. |
| **Gradient** | A vector of partial derivatives pointing in the direction of steepest ascent. |
| **Hessian Matrix** | A square matrix of second-order partial derivatives used in second-derivative tests. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:** Almost every engineering optimization problem — minimising fuel consumption, maximising structural strength, minimising cost — ultimately reduces to finding the *best* values of a *function of one or two variables*. Hence, mastery of this topic is a prerequisite for all subsequent modules (linear programming, non-linear programming, dynamic programming).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functions of a Single Variable — Deep Analysis

A function $y = f(x)$ in optimisation is analysed using the following key concepts:

1. **Continuity at a point $x_0$**:
   $f$ is continuous at $x_0$ if $\displaystyle\lim_{x \to x_0} f(x) = f(x_0)$.

2. **Differentiability at $x_0$**:
   The derivative $f'(x_0)$ exists if the limit $\displaystyle\lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(x_0+h) - f(x_0)}{h}$ exists and is finite.

3. **Stationary / Critical Point**:
   A point $x^{\ast}$ where $f'(x^{\ast}) = 0$ (first-order necessary condition).

4. **Second-Derivative Test**:
   - If $f''(x^{\ast}) > 0$ → **Local Minimum**
   - If $f''(x^{\ast}) < 0$ → **Local Maximum**
   - If $f''(x^{\ast}) = 0$ → **Inconclusive** (higher-order test required)

5. **Convexity (Single Variable)**:
   A function is *convex* on an interval if, for any two points $x_1, x_2$ and $\lambda \in [0,1]$:
   $$f\bigl(\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda)x_2\bigr) \leq \lambda f(x_1) + (1-\lambda) f(x_2)$$
   A convex function has the property that any stationary point is a **global minimum**.

### 2.2 Functions of Two Variables — Deep Analysis

For $z = f(x, y)$, we must consider the behaviour in a *two-dimensional plane* (the $xy$-plane). The major analytical tools are:

1. **Partial Derivative with respect to $x$** (treat $y$ as a constant):
   $$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x} = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(x+h, y) - f(x, y)}{h}$$

2. **Partial Derivative with respect to $y$** (treat $x$ as a constant):
   $$\frac{\partial f}{\partial y} = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(x, y+h) - f(x, y)}{h}$$

3. **First-Order Necessary Condition (FONC)**:
   At a stationary point $(x^{\ast}, y^{\ast})$:
   $$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\bigg|_{(x^{\ast}, y^{\ast})} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{\partial f}{\partial y}\bigg|_{(x^{\ast}, y^{\ast})} = 0$$

4. **Second-Order Partial Derivatives**:
   $$f_{xx} = \frac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x^{2}}, \quad f_{yy} = \frac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial y^{2}}, \quad f_{xy} = f_{yx} = \frac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x \, \partial y}$$

5. **Hessian Matrix**:
   $$H = \begin{bmatrix} f_{xx} & f_{xy} \\ f_{yx} & f_{yy} \end{bmatrix}$$

6. **Second-Order Sufficient Condition (SOSC)** — Let $D = f_{xx} f_{yy} - (f_{xy})^{2}$ (the determinant of the Hessian).
   - If $D > 0$ and $f_{xx} > 0$ → **Local Minimum**
   - If $D > 0$ and $f_{xx} < 0$ → **Local Maximum**
   - If $D < 0$ → **Saddle Point**
   - If $D = 0$ → **Inconclusive**

7. **Convexity (Two Variables)**:
   A function $f(x, y)$ is convex on a convex set $S$ if its Hessian $H(x, y)$ is *positive semi-definite* for all $(x, y) \in S$. A *strictly convex* function has a *unique global minimum*.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Concept** | **Single Variable** | **Two Variables** |
|---|---|---|
| Function notation | $f(x)$ | $f(x, y)$ |
| First derivative | $f'(x) = \dfrac{df}{dx}$ | $f_{x}, f_{y}$ (partials) |
| Stationary condition | $f'(x^{\ast}) = 0$ | $f_{x} = 0,\ f_{y} = 0$ |
| Second derivative | $f''(x^{\ast})$ | Hessian $H = \begin{bmatrix} f_{xx} & f_{xy} \\ f_{yx} & f_{yy} \end{bmatrix}$ |
| Test quantity | $f''(x^{\ast})$ | $D = f_{xx}f_{yy} - f_{xy}^{2}$ |
| Local Min condition | $f'(x^{\ast})=0,\ f''>0$ | $D>0,\ f_{xx}>0$ |
| Local Max condition | $f'(x^{\ast})=0,\ f''<0$ | $D>0,\ f_{xx}<0$ |
| Saddle point | N/A | $D < 0$ |
| Convexity test | $f''(x) \geq 0\ \forall x$ | $H$ is positive semi-definite |
| Gradient vector | $f'(x)$ | $\nabla f = (f_{x},\ f_{y})^{T}$ |
| Directional derivative | $f'(x) \cdot u$ | $\nabla f \cdot \hat{u}$ |
| Tangent plane | $y = f(a) + f'(a)(x-a)$ | $z = f(a,b) + f_{x}(x-a) + f_{y}(y-b)$ |
| Total differential | $df = f'(x)\,dx$ | $df = f_{x}\,dx + f_{y}\,dy$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> - *Civil/Structural Engineering:* Minimising material cost for a beam — function of cross-section $b, d$.
> - *Electrical Engineering:* Maximising power transfer — function of load resistance $R$.
> - *Mechanical Engineering:* Minimising drag coefficient — function of Mach number and angle of attack.
> - *Economics:* Maximising profit — function of production quantity and unit price.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Derivation 1: Stationary Point of a Single-Variable Function

**Problem:** Find the stationary point of $f(x) = x^{3} - 6x^{2} + 9x + 2$ and classify it.

**Step 1 — Compute the first derivative.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(x) &= x^{3} - 6x^{2} + 9x + 2 \\
f'(x) &= \frac{d}{dx}\bigl(x^{3}\bigr) - 6\cdot\frac{d}{dx}\bigl(x^{2}\bigr) + 9\cdot\frac{d}{dx}(x) + \frac{d}{dx}(2) \\
f'(x) &= 3x^{2} - 12x + 9
\end{aligned}
$$

Here, the power rule $\frac{d}{dx}(x^{n}) = nx^{n-1}$ was applied term-by-term, and the constant rule $\frac{d}{dx}(c) = 0$ was used on the term $2$.

**Step 2 — Apply the first-order necessary condition $f'(x^{\ast}) = 0$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
3x^{2} - 12x + 9 &= 0 \\
3(x^{2} - 4x + 3) &= 0 \\
3(x-1)(x-3) &= 0 \\
\Rightarrow x^{\ast} &= 1 \quad \text{or} \quad x^{\ast} = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

We factor the quadratic by splitting the middle term: $x^{2} - 4x + 3 = (x-1)(x-3)$.

**Step 3 — Compute the second derivative to classify the stationary points.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f''(x) &= \frac{d}{dx}\bigl(3x^{2} - 12x + 9\bigr) = 6x - 12
\end{aligned}
$$

We differentiate $f'(x)$ term-by-term using the power rule.

**Step 4 — Evaluate $f''(x)$ at each critical point.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f''(1) &= 6(1) - 12 = -6 < 0 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Local MAXIMUM at } x = 1 \\
f''(3) &= 6(3) - 12 = 6 > 0 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Local MINIMUM at } x = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

The sign of $f''$ tells us the curvature: negative curvature = peak, positive curvature = valley.

**Step 5 — Compute the function values at the critical points (optional but recommended in KTU).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(1) &= 1 - 6 + 9 + 2 = 6 \\
f(3) &= 27 - 54 + 27 + 2 = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** Local maximum at $(1, 6)$; Local minimum at $(3, 2)$.

---

### 3.2 Worked Derivation 2: Stationary Point of a Two-Variable Function

**Problem:** Find and classify the stationary point of $f(x, y) = x^{2} + y^{2} - 4x + 6y + 13$.

**Step 1 — Compute the first-order partial derivatives.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{x} &= \frac{\partial f}{\partial x} = 2x - 4 \\
f_{y} &= \frac{\partial f}{\partial y} = 2y + 6
\end{aligned}
$$

When differentiating with respect to $x$, $y$ is treated as a constant, and vice-versa.

**Step 2 — Apply the first-order necessary conditions $f_x = 0$ and $f_y = 0$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
2x - 4 &= 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x^{\ast} = 2 \\
2y + 6 &= 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad y^{\ast} = -3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the second-order partial derivatives (for the Hessian).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{xx} &= \frac{\partial}{\partial x}(2x - 4) = 2 \\
f_{yy} &= \frac{\partial}{\partial y}(2y + 6) = 2 \\
f_{xy} &= \frac{\partial}{\partial y}(2x - 4) = 0 \\
f_{yx} &= \frac{\partial}{\partial x}(2y + 6) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

By **Clairaut's Theorem** (equality of mixed partials), $f_{xy} = f_{yx}$ for continuously differentiable functions.

**Step 4 — Construct the Hessian Matrix at $(2, -3)$.**

$$
H(2, -3) = \begin{bmatrix} f_{xx} & f_{xy} \\ f_{yx} & f_{yy} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the determinant $D$ of the Hessian.**

$$
D = f_{xx}\,f_{yy} - (f_{xy})^{2} = (2)(2) - (0)^{2} = 4
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the Second-Order Sufficient Condition.**

Since $D = 4 > 0$ and $f_{xx} = 2 > 0$, the point $(2, -3)$ is a **strict local minimum**.

**Step 7 — Compute the function value at the stationary point.**

$$
f(2, -3) = (2)^{2} + (-3)^{2} - 4(2) + 6(-3) + 13 = 4 + 9 - 8 - 18 + 13 = 0
$$

**Final Answer:** $f$ has a **local (in fact, global) minimum** of $0$ at $(x^{\ast}, y^{\ast}) = (2, -3)$.

---

### 3.3 Worked Derivation 3: Concluding the Global Nature of a Minimum

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The function $f(x,y) = x^{2} + y^{2} - 4x + 6y + 13$ can be rewritten by **completing the square** to prove its global minimum rigorously.

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(x, y) &= (x^{2} - 4x) + (y^{2} + 6y) + 13 \\
&= (x^{2} - 4x + 4) + (y^{2} + 6y + 9) + 13 - 4 - 9 \\
&= (x - 2)^{2} + (y + 3)^{2} + 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $(x-2)^{2} \geq 0$ and $(y+3)^{2} \geq 0$ for all real $x, y$, the minimum value of $f$ is $0$, attained uniquely at $(2, -3)$. This confirms the Hessian test result.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Classification of Optimisation Landscapes

The following **Mermaid flowchart** depicts how a function's behaviour is categorised in a typical KTU 2024 Module 1 problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Function f of n variables] --> B{n = 1 or n = 2}
    B -- Single Variable --> C[Compute f-prime of x]
    B -- Two Variables --> D[Compute partials f-x and f-y]
    C --> E{f-prime equals zero}
    D --> F{f-x = 0 AND f-y = 0}
    E -- No --> G[No Stationary Point in Domain]
    E -- Yes --> H[Classify via f-double-prime]
    F -- No --> G
    F -- Yes --> I[Compute Hessian H]
    I --> J[Determinant D = f-xx f-yy - f-xy squared]
    J --> K{D sign analysis}
    H --> L[Sign of f-double-prime]
    L -- Positive --> M[Local MINIMUM]
    L -- Negative --> N[Local MAXIMUM]
    L -- Zero --> O[Higher Order Test]
    K -- D greater than 0 and f-xx greater than 0 --> M
    K -- D greater than 0 and f-xx less than 0 --> N
    K -- D less than 0 --> P[SADDLE POINT]
    K -- D equals 0 --> O
    M --> Q[If convex then GLOBAL minimum]
    N --> R[If concave then GLOBAL maximum]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Optimisation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[INPUT: Objective Function f of x comma y] --> A2[Step 1: Domain Identification]
    A2 --> A3[Step 2: Differentiability Check]
    A3 --> A4[Step 3: Compute Gradient]
    A4 --> A5[Step 4: Solve Stationary Equations]
    A5 --> A6[Step 5: Compute Hessian Matrix]
    A6 --> A7[Step 6: Apply Second-Order Test]
    A7 --> A8[Step 7: Classify Stationary Point]
    A8 --> A9[OUTPUT: Optimum Value and Location]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Visualisation of Stationary Points in 2D

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ZONE_A[Local Maximum Region]
        M1[Peak: f-xx less than 0, D greater than 0]
    end
    subgraph ZONE_B[Local Minimum Region]
        M2[Valley: f-xx greater than 0, D greater than 0]
    end
    subgraph ZONE_C[Saddle Point Region]
        M3[Pass between two peaks: D less than 0]
    end
    subgraph ZONE_D[Inconclusive Region]
        M4[Test fails: D equals 0]
    end
    M1 --> CONCLUSION[Apply Convexity Test for Global Optimum]
    M2 --> CONCLUSION
    M3 --> CONCLUSION
    M4 --> CONCLUSION
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:**
> - In *ZONE_A*, the surface curves *downward* in every direction — a peak.
> - In *ZONE_B*, the surface curves *upward* in every direction — a valley.
> - In *ZONE_C*, the surface curves up in one direction and down in another — a saddle.
> - In *ZONE_D*, the standard second-order test is inconclusive; one must use higher-order derivatives or numerical methods.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**

**Q1. Define a stationary point of a function of two variables. State the first-order necessary condition for a point to be a stationary point.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **stationary point** of a function $f(x, y)$ is a point $(x^{\ast}, y^{\ast})$ in the domain of $f$ where the function's value ceases to change to first order; that is, the tangent plane to the surface is horizontal.

The **First-Order Necessary Condition (FONC)** for an unconstrained optimum (assuming differentiability) is that the gradient vector vanishes:

$$\nabla f(x^{\ast}, y^{\ast}) = \begin{bmatrix} f_{x}(x^{\ast}, y^{\ast}) \\ f_{y}(x^{\ast}, y^{\ast}) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

This means $f_{x} = 0$ and $f_{y} = 0$ simultaneously at $(x^{\ast}, y^{\ast})$. **(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for the FONC statement.)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**

**Q2. What is a Hessian matrix? Write the Hessian of $f(x, y) = x^{3} + y^{3} - 3xy$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Hessian matrix** of a twice-differentiable scalar function $f: \mathbb{R}^{2} \to \mathbb{R}$ is the $2 \times 2$ matrix of all second-order partial derivatives, used in the second-derivative test for classifying stationary points.

For $f(x, y) = x^{3} + y^{3} - 3xy$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{x} &= 3x^{2} - 3y \quad &\Rightarrow\quad f_{xx} &= 6x,\quad f_{xy} = -3 \\
f_{y} &= 3y^{2} - 3x \quad &\Rightarrow\quad f_{yy} &= 6y,\quad f_{yx} = -3
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
H = \begin{bmatrix} 6x & -3 \\ -3 & 6y \end{bmatrix}
$$

**(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for the Hessian.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — End Semester, Module 1]**

---

#### ✅ **Question A (Choice 1)** — 14 Marks

**(a)** Find the local extremum of $f(x) = 2x^{3} - 15x^{2} + 36x + 10$ using the second-derivative test. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Find and classify the stationary points of $f(x, y) = x^{3} + y^{3} - 3xy$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to A(a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Compute $f'(x)$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f'(x) &= 6x^{2} - 30x + 36
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Set $f'(x) = 0$ and solve.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
6x^{2} - 30x + 36 &= 0 \\
6(x^{2} - 5x + 6) &= 0 \\
6(x-2)(x-3) &= 0 \\
\Rightarrow x_{1} &= 2,\quad x_{2} = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $f''(x)$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f''(x) &= 12x - 30
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Classify.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f''(2) &= 24 - 30 = -6 < 0 \Rightarrow \text{Local MAXIMUM at } x = 2 \\
f''(3) &= 36 - 30 = 6 > 0 \Rightarrow \text{Local MINIMUM at } x = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute extreme values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(2) &= 16 - 60 + 72 + 10 = 38 \quad \text{(Local Max value)} \\
f(3) &= 54 - 135 + 108 + 10 = 37 \quad \text{(Local Min value)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Local maximum of $38$ at $x = 2$; Local minimum of $37$ at $x = 3$. **(7 marks — 2 for derivative, 2 for solving, 2 for classification, 1 for values.)**

---

**Solution to A(b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Compute first-order partials.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{x} &= 3x^{2} - 3y \\
f_{y} &= 3y^{2} - 3x
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Set $f_{x} = 0$ and $f_{y} = 0$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
3x^{2} - 3y &= 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad y = x^{2} \\
3y^{2} - 3x &= 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = y^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve the simultaneous equations.** Substituting $y = x^{2}$ into $x = y^{2}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= (x^{2})^{2} = x^{4} \\
x^{4} - x &= 0 \\
x(x^{3} - 1) &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

This gives $x = 0$ or $x^{3} = 1 \Rightarrow x = 1$.

Corresponding $y$ values: For $x=0, y = 0^{2} = 0$. For $x=1, y = 1^{2} = 1$.

So the stationary points are $(0, 0)$ and $(1, 1)$.

**Step 4 — Compute second-order partials.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{xx} &= 6x,\quad f_{yy} = 6y,\quad f_{xy} = -3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Construct Hessian and compute $D$ at each point.**

$$
H = \begin{bmatrix} 6x & -3 \\ -3 & 6y \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = (6x)(6y) - (-3)^{2} = 36xy - 9
$$

**At $(0, 0)$:**

$$
D = 36(0)(0) - 9 = -9 < 0
$$

Since $D < 0$, the point $(0, 0)$ is a **SADDLE POINT**.

**At $(1, 1)$:**

$$
D = 36(1)(1) - 9 = 27 > 0
$$

And $f_{xx}(1, 1) = 6 > 0$. So $(1, 1)$ is a **LOCAL MINIMUM**.

**Step 6 — Compute the function value at $(1, 1)$:**

$$
f(1, 1) = 1 + 1 - 3 = -1
$$

**Result:** Saddle point at $(0, 0)$; Local minimum of $-1$ at $(1, 1)$. **(7 marks — 2 for partials, 2 for solving, 2 for Hessian test, 1 for function value.)**

---

#### ✅ **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)** — 14 Marks

**(a)** Find the absolute maximum and minimum of $f(x) = x^{3} - 3x + 2$ on the closed interval $[-2, 3]$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A rectangular box with an open top is to have a surface area of $108 \text{ in}^{2}$. Find the dimensions that yield the maximum volume. Use the second-derivative test. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to B(a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Compute $f'(x)$ and find critical points.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f'(x) &= 3x^{2} - 3 = 3(x^{2} - 1) = 3(x-1)(x+1) \\
f'(x) = 0 &\Rightarrow x = 1,\ x = -1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Both lie in $[-2, 3]$. Compute $f''(x)$.**

$$
f''(x) = 6x
$$

**Step 3 — Classify interior critical points.**

$$
f''(1) = 6 > 0 \Rightarrow \text{Local Min at } x = 1; \quad f''(-1) = -6 < 0 \Rightarrow \text{Local Max at } x = -1
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate $f$ at all critical points and endpoints.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(-2) &= -8 + 6 + 2 = 0 \\
f(-1) &= -1 + 3 + 2 = 4 \quad \text{(Local Max)} \\
f(1) &= 1 - 3 + 2 = 0 \quad \text{(Local Min)} \\
f(3) &= 27 - 9 + 2 = 20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:**
- **Absolute Maximum** $= 20$ at $x = 3$ (endpoint, not a critical point!).
- **Absolute Minimum** $= 0$ occurring at both $x = -2$ and $x = 1$.

**(7 marks — 2 for derivative, 2 for critical points, 2 for endpoint evaluation, 1 for final classification.)**

---

**Solution to B(b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Set up the problem.** Let length $= x$, width $= y$, height $= z$. Open top surface area constraint:

$$
xy + 2xz + 2yz = 108
$$

Volume to be maximised: $V = xyz$.

**Step 2 — Solve the constraint for $z$ and substitute into $V$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
z(2x + 2y) &= 108 - xy \\
z &= \frac{108 - xy}{2(x+y)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
V(x, y) = xy \cdot \frac{108 - xy}{2(x+y)} = \frac{108xy - x^{2}y^{2}}{2(x+y)}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply partial derivatives and set to zero.**

By symmetry of the box (rectangular, open top), the optimum occurs at $x = y$. Set $x = y = a$:

$$
V = \frac{108 a^{2} - a^{4}}{2(2a)} = \frac{108a - a^{3}}{4} = 27a - \frac{a^{3}}{4}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $V'(a)$ and set to zero.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V'(a) &= 27 - \frac{3a^{2}}{4} = 0 \\
a^{2} &= 36 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = 6 \text{ (positive)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply second-derivative test.**

$$
V''(a) = -\frac{6a}{4} = -\frac{3a}{2}
$$

$$
V''(6) = -\frac{18}{2} = -9 < 0 \quad \Rightarrow \text{MAXIMUM}
$$

**Step 6 — Find $z$.**

$$
z = \frac{108 - 36}{2(12)} = \frac{72}{24} = 3
$$

**Result:** Box dimensions: $x = 6$ in, $y = 6$ in, $z = 3$ in. **Maximum volume** $V = 6 \times 6 \times 3 = 108 \text{ in}^{3}$. **(7 marks — 2 for setup, 2 for derivative, 1 for second-derivative test, 2 for dimensions and volume.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to AVOID:**
> 1. **Forgetting to check endpoints** in single-variable closed-interval problems — endpoints frequently give the absolute max/min (as seen in Q-B(a) where the absolute max is at $x=3$, NOT a critical point). **Loss of up to 2 marks.**
> 2. **Using the Hessian determinant formula incorrectly** — students often write $D = f_{xx} f_{yy} + f_{xy}^{2}$ instead of $D = f_{xx} f_{yy} - f_{xy}^{2}$. The *minus* sign is critical. **Loss of 2-3 marks.**
> 3. **Confusing saddle points with minima/maxima** — if $D < 0$, it is *neither*; it is a saddle. Do not blindly report "minimum". **Loss of 1-2 marks.**
> 4. **Failing to state the FONC explicitly** — KTU examiners award marks for the *statement* of necessary conditions, not just the calculation. Always write: "For an interior extremum, $f_x = 0$ and $f_y = 0$."
> 5. **Skipping the function value computation** — even if classification is correct, KTU typically asks for the *optimum value*, not just the location.
> 6. **Two-variable problems: not verifying $f_{xy} = f_{yx}$** — KTU expects you to mention Clairaut's theorem (or symmetry) to justify that the Hessian is symmetric.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Functions of Single and Two Variables**

- ✅ A function $f: D \to \mathbb{R}$ maps inputs to a *single* real output.
- ✅ **Single-variable** functions are analysed using $f'(x)$ and $f''(x)$.
- ✅ **Two-variable** functions are analysed using partial derivatives, the gradient, and the Hessian.
- ✅ **FONC for unconstrained optimum:** $f_x = 0$ and $f_y = 0$ (two-variable) or $f'(x) = 0$ (single-variable).
- ✅ **Hessian matrix** is a $2 \times 2$ symmetric matrix of second-order partials: $H = \begin{bmatrix} f_{xx} & f_{xy} \\ f_{yx} & f_{yy} \end{bmatrix}$.
- ✅ **Test quantity** for two variables: $D = f_{xx} f_{yy} - (f_{xy})^{2}$.
- ✅ **Classification rules** (memorise!):
  - $D > 0,\ f_{xx} > 0$ → **Local Min**
  - $D > 0,\ f_{xx} < 0$ → **Local Max**
  - $D < 0$ → **Saddle Point**
  - $D = 0$ → **Inconclusive**
- ✅ **Convex function** ⇒ any stationary point is a *global* minimum.
- ✅ **Concave function** ⇒ any stationary point is a *global* maximum.
- ✅ **Clairaut's Theorem** guarantees $f_{xy} = f_{yx}$ for continuously differentiable $f$.
- ✅ **Gradient** $\nabla f = (f_x, f_y)$ points in the direction of steepest *ascent*.
- ✅ **Closed-interval problems** require evaluation at both *critical points* AND *endpoints*.
- ✅ The **second-derivative test** is sufficient but not necessary — when it fails ($D=0$), use higher-order derivatives or rewrite by completing the square.
- ✅ Always quote the **optimum location** AND the **optimum value** in your final answer.
- ✅ For application problems (like the box problem), the constraint must be substituted into the objective *before* differentiation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Convexity and Concavity of Functions - Definition of Global and Local Optima - Optimality Criteria
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT723 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Convexity and Concavity of Functions - Definition of Global and Local Optima - Optimality Criteria

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Convex Function — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Standard Terminology)**
> A function $f: \mathbb{R}^n \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ is said to be **convex** on a convex set $S \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ if for any two points $x, y \in S$ and for any scalar $\lambda \in [0, 1]$, the following inequality holds:
> $$f(\lambda x + (1-\lambda)y) \leq \lambda f(x) + (1-\lambda) f(y)$$

If the inequality is strict for all $x \neq y$ and $\lambda \in (0, 1)$, the function is called **strictly convex**.

**Geometric Interpretation:** The line segment (chord) connecting any two points on the graph of the function lies **on or above** the graph itself. In other words, the graph of the function always "bows upward" or stays flat — never dipping below the chord.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Engineering Meaning:** Convex optimization problems are the *gold standard* in engineering design because every local minimum is guaranteed to be a global minimum. This makes them computationally tractable and reliable.

---

### 1.2 Concave Function — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Standard Terminology)**
> A function $f: \mathbb{R}^n \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ is said to be **concave** on a convex set $S \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ if for any two points $x, y \in S$ and for any scalar $\lambda \in [0, 1]$, the following inequality holds:
> $$f(\lambda x + (1-\lambda)y) \geq \lambda f(x) + (1-\lambda) f(y)$$

If the inequality is strict, the function is called **strictly concave**.

**Geometric Interpretation:** The chord connecting any two points on the graph lies **on or below** the graph. The function "bows downward."

---

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Bowl vs. Mountain" Picture**
>
> - **Convex Function** = A **bowl** or **valley**. If you place a ball anywhere inside, it rolls to the **single lowest point** (global minimum). The shape *holds* everything safely. Example: $f(x) = x^2$, $f(x) = e^x$ for $x \geq 0$, cost-of-production curves, potential energy wells in physics.
>
> - **Concave Function** = An **upside-down bowl** or **mountain peak**. A ball placed on it rolls down on either side away from the peak. Example: $f(x) = -x^2$, $f(x) = \sqrt{x}$ for $x \geq 0$, profit functions under perfect competition, utility-of-consumption curves.
>
> - **Neither Convex nor Concave** = A **wavy terrain** with multiple hills and valleys. Optimization becomes tricky because the ball might get stuck in a *local* valley and miss the *global* deepest one.

---

### 1.4 Global vs. Local Optima — Definitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Global Optimum (Minimum)**
> A point $x^* \in S$ is a **global minimum** of $f$ on $S$ if:
> $$f(x^*) \leq f(x) \quad \forall x \in S$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Local Optimum (Minimum)**
> A point $x^* \in S$ is a **local minimum** of $f$ on $S$ if there exists a neighbourhood $N(x^*, \delta) = \{x : \vert x - x^* \vert < \delta\}$ such that:
> $$f(x^*) \leq f(x) \quad \forall x \in S \cap N(x^*, \delta)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Strict variants** replace "$\leq$" with "$<$" for $x \neq x^*$. Similarly, replace "minimum" with "maximum" and reverse the inequality for global/local **maximum** definitions.

**Key Distinction:**
- **Local Optimum** = Best in a *small neighbourhood* (a hilltop in a mountain range).
- **Global Optimum** = Best *everywhere* in the domain (the highest peak in the entire world).
- For **convex** minimization problems, every local minimum is a global minimum — this is the most important theorem in convex optimization.

---

### 1.5 Optimality Criteria — Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Optimality Criterion (Informal Statement)**
> Optimality criteria are the **mathematical tests (conditions)** we apply to a candidate point $x^*$ to determine whether it is a local/global minimizer, maximizer, or saddle point of the objective function. For unconstrained problems, these criteria involve the **gradient** (first-order) and **Hessian matrix** (second-order).

**The two pillars of unconstrained optimality:**
1. **First-Order Necessary Condition (FONC):** $\nabla f(x^*) = 0$ — the slope must be flat.
2. **Second-Order Conditions (SONC / SOSC):** Involve the Hessian matrix $H(x^*)$ being positive semi-definite (necessary for min) or positive definite (sufficient for min).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Algebraic Properties of Convexity/Concavity

**Property 1 — Linearity Preservation:**
- The **negative** of a convex function is **concave**, and vice versa.
- Mathematically: $f$ convex $\iff -f$ concave.

**Property 2 — Sum Rule:**
- The **non-negative weighted sum** of convex functions is convex.
- If $f_1, f_2$ are convex, then $f_1 + f_2$ is convex.

**Property 3 — Scalar Multiplication:**
- If $f$ is convex and $\alpha \geq 0$, then $\alpha f$ is convex.

**Property 4 — Composition:**
- If $f$ is convex and $g$ is convex and **non-decreasing**, then $g(f(x))$ is convex.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** These properties are essential for *recognizing* convexity in engineering problems (e.g., verifying that an objective function is convex so that gradient descent will converge to the global optimum).

---

### 2.2 Second-Order (Hessian) Test for Convexity

For a **twice continuously differentiable** function $f: \mathbb{R}^n \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$:

- $f$ is **convex** on an open convex set $S$ $\iff$ the **Hessian matrix** $H(x) \succeq 0$ (positive semi-definite) for all $x \in S$.
- $f$ is **strictly convex** on $S$ if $H(x) \succ 0$ (positive definite) for all $x \in S$.
- $f$ is **concave** on $S$ $\iff H(x) \preceq 0$ (negative semi-definite) for all $x \in S$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note:** Positive semi-definiteness of the Hessian is a *sufficient* (not necessary) condition for *strict* convexity. There exist strictly convex functions whose Hessian is *not* positive definite everywhere (e.g., $f(x) = x^4$ at $x = 0$).

---

### 2.3 Unconstrained Optimality — Complete Framework

**Stationary Point:** A point where $\nabla f(x^*) = \mathbf{0}$ is called a **stationary point** (or critical point). Stationary points can be local minima, local maxima, or saddle points — we need second-order conditions to distinguish.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Mathematical Statement | Condition Type | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Convex Function | $f(\lambda x + (1-\lambda)y) \leq \lambda f(x) + (1-\lambda) f(y)$ | Definition | $\lambda \in [0,1]$ |
| 2 | Concave Function | $f(\lambda x + (1-\lambda)y) \geq \lambda f(x) + (1-\lambda) f(y)$ | Definition | $\lambda \in [0,1]$ |
| 3 | Hessian Test (Convex) | $H(x) = \nabla^2 f(x) \succeq 0$ | 2nd-order | For $\mathcal{C}^2$ functions |
| 4 | Hessian Test (Concave) | $H(x) = \nabla^2 f(x) \preceq 0$ | 2nd-order | For $\mathcal{C}^2$ functions |
| 5 | Global Minimum | $f(x^*) \leq f(x),\ \forall x \in S$ | Definition | Domain-wide |
| 6 | Local Minimum | $f(x^*) \leq f(x),\ \forall x \in N(x^*,\delta) \cap S$ | Definition | Neighbourhood |
| 7 | FONC (Necessary) | $\nabla f(x^*) = \mathbf{0}$ | 1st-order | Holds at every local extremum |
| 8 | SONC (Necessary) | $\nabla f(x^*) = \mathbf{0}$ and $H(x^*) \succeq 0$ | 2nd-order | Required for local min |
| 9 | SOSC (Sufficient) | $\nabla f(x^*) = \mathbf{0}$ and $H(x^*) \succ 0$ | 2nd-order | Guarantees strict local min |
| 10 | Max SONC | $\nabla f(x^*) = \mathbf{0}$ and $H(x^*) \preceq 0$ | 2nd-order | Required for local max |
| 11 | Saddle Point | $H(x^*)$ **indefinite** | 2nd-order | Neither min nor max |
| 12 | Convex Min Theorem | Local min $=$ Global min | Theorem | Holds only for convex $f$ |

### 2.5 Eigenvalue Test for Hessian

Since positive/negative definiteness of $H$ is equivalent to all eigenvalues being positive/negative:

- $H \succ 0$ (positive definite) $\iff \lambda_i(H) > 0$ for all $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$.
- $H \succeq 0$ (positive semi-definite) $\iff \lambda_i(H) \geq 0$ for all $i$.
- $H \prec 0$ (negative definite) $\iff \lambda_i(H) < 0$ for all $i$.
- $H$ indefinite $\iff$ eigenvalues have mixed signs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Application:** In **structural optimization**, convexity of the objective (e.g., compliance, weight) and convexity of the feasible set (design constraints) guarantee that iterative algorithms like the Method of Moving Asymptotes (MMA) converge to a global optimum — critical in **topology optimization** of trusses and aerospace components.

---

### 2.6 Why These Criteria Matter — Engineering Insight

- In **machine learning**, the loss function $J(\theta)$ is designed to be convex so that gradient descent finds the *global* minimum (e.g., linear regression, SVM).
- In **optimal control**, the cost functional being convex enables efficient solution via Pontryagin's Maximum Principle.
- In **economics**, consumer utility functions are typically **concave** (diminishing marginal utility), justifying the concavity assumption in utility maximization problems.
- In **portfolio optimization** (Markowitz model), the variance function is convex, making it amenable to quadratic programming solvers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of the First-Order Necessary Condition (FONC)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (FONC):** If $f: \mathbb{R}^n \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ is continuously differentiable and $x^*$ is a local minimum, then $\nabla f(x^*) = \mathbf{0}$.

**Proof Sketch:**

Consider an arbitrary direction $d \in \mathbb{R}^n$ and the scalar function $\phi(\alpha) = f(x^* + \alpha d)$ defined for small $\alpha$.

- Since $x^*$ is a local minimum, $\phi(\alpha) \geq \phi(0) = f(x^*)$ for all sufficiently small $\alpha$.
- Therefore $\alpha = 0$ is a local minimum of $\phi(\alpha)$.
- By the single-variable necessary condition, $\phi'(0) = 0$.
- Applying the chain rule: $\phi'(\alpha) = \nabla f(x^* + \alpha d)^T d$.
- Setting $\alpha = 0$: $\phi'(0) = \nabla f(x^*)^T d = 0$.
- Since this must hold for **every** direction $d$, we conclude $\nabla f(x^*) = \mathbf{0}$. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Second-Order Conditions

**Theorem (SONC — Necessary Condition for Local Minimum):**
If $f$ is twice continuously differentiable ($\mathcal{C}^2$) and $x^*$ is a local minimum, then:
- $\nabla f(x^*) = \mathbf{0}$
- $H(x^*) \succeq 0$ (positive semi-definite)

**Proof Sketch:**

For any direction $d$, consider the Taylor expansion of $\phi(\alpha) = f(x^* + \alpha d)$ around $\alpha = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi(\alpha) &= \phi(0) + \alpha \phi'(0) + \frac{\alpha^2}{2} \phi''(0) + O(\alpha^3)
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\phi'(0) = \nabla f(x^*)^T d = 0$ (by FONC), we have:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi(\alpha) - \phi(0) &= \frac{\alpha^2}{2} \phi''(0) + O(\alpha^3)
\end{aligned}
$$

For $x^*$ to be a local minimum, $\phi(\alpha) - \phi(0) \geq 0$ for small $\alpha$. This forces:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi''(0) &= d^T H(x^*) d \geq 0 \quad \forall d \in \mathbb{R}^n
\end{aligned}
$$

The condition $d^T H(x^*) d \geq 0$ for all $d$ is precisely the definition of **positive semi-definiteness** of $H(x^*)$. $\blacksquare$

**Theorem (SOSC — Sufficient Condition for Strict Local Minimum):**
If $f$ is $\mathcal{C}^2$ at $x^*$, and if $\nabla f(x^*) = \mathbf{0}$ with $H(x^*) \succ 0$ (positive definite), then $x^*$ is a **strict local minimum**.

**Proof Sketch:**

By the second-order Taylor expansion with Lagrange remainder, for $x$ near $x^*$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(x) - f(x^*) &= \nabla f(x^*)^T (x - x^*) + \frac{1}{2}(x - x^*)^T H(x^*)(x - x^*) + o(\vert x - x^* \vert^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

The first term vanishes since $\nabla f(x^*) = \mathbf{0}$. For sufficiently small $\vert x - x^* \vert$, the dominant term is the quadratic form $\frac{1}{2}(x-x^*)^T H(x^*)(x-x^*)$. Since $H(x^*) \succ 0$, by the **Rayleigh quotient** theorem:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(x-x^*)^T H(x^*)(x-x^*) &\geq \lambda_{\min}(H(x^*)) \vert x - x^* \vert^2 > 0 \quad \text{for } x \neq x^*
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\lambda_{\min}(H(x^*)) > 0$ is the smallest eigenvalue. Hence $f(x) > f(x^*)$ for $x$ sufficiently close to $x^*$, proving strict local minimality. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Comprehensive Worked Example

**Problem:** Analyze the function $f(x, y) = x^3 + y^3 - 3xy$ for optima. Classify the nature of the critical points.

**Step 1 — Compute the Gradient (First-Order Conditions):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial f}{\partial x} &= 3x^2 - 3y \\
\frac{\partial f}{\partial y} &= 3y^2 - 3x
\end{aligned}
$$

Setting $\nabla f = \mathbf{0}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
3x^2 - 3y &= 0 \implies y = x^2 \\
3y^2 - 3x &= 0 \implies x = y^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve the System:**

Substituting $y = x^2$ into $x = y^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= (x^2)^2 = x^4 \\
x^4 - x &= 0 \\
x(x^3 - 1) &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

So $x = 0$ or $x = 1$.

Corresponding points:
- $x = 0 \implies y = 0^2 = 0 \implies (0, 0)$
- $x = 1 \implies y = 1^2 = 1 \implies (1, 1)$

**Step 3 — Compute the Hessian Matrix:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(x,y) &= \begin{bmatrix} \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x^2} & \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x \partial y} \\[6pt] \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial y \partial x} & \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial y^2} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 6x & -3 \\ -3 & 6y \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Classify Each Critical Point Using Hessian Definiteness:**

**At $(0, 0)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(0,0) &= \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -3 \\ -3 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Eigenvalues: $\det(H - \lambda I) = \lambda^2 - 9 = 0 \implies \lambda = \pm 3$.

- One eigenvalue positive, one negative $\implies H(0,0)$ is **indefinite**.
- $\therefore (0, 0)$ is a **Saddle Point**.

**At $(1, 1)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(1,1) &= \begin{bmatrix} 6 & -3 \\ -3 & 6 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Eigenvalues: $\det(H - \lambda I) = (6 - \lambda)^2 - 9 = 0 \implies \lambda^2 - 12\lambda + 27 = 0$.

Solving: $\lambda = \frac{12 \pm \sqrt{144 - 108}}{2} = \frac{12 \pm 6}{2} = 9$ or $3$.

- Both eigenvalues positive $\implies H(1,1)$ is **positive definite**.
- $\therefore (1, 1)$ is a **Strict Local Minimum**.

The minimum value is $f(1, 1) = 1 + 1 - 3 = -1$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** This is a classic KTU-type problem. Always classify **all** critical points — partial credit is given for correctly finding critical points even if the Hessian classification has an error.

### 3.4 Single-Variable Special Case

For $f: \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ (single variable) twice differentiable:

- **Necessary for local min:** $f'(x^*) = 0$ and $f''(x^*) \geq 0$.
- **Sufficient for strict local min:** $f'(x^*) = 0$ and $f''(x^*) > 0$.
- **Saddle point (inflection):** $f'(x^*) = 0$ and $f''(x^*) = 0$ (requires higher-order test).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common KTU Trap:** $f''(x^*) = 0$ does **NOT** mean it is a minimum! It only means the second-order test is *inconclusive*. A higher-order derivative test or graphical analysis is required.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block-Level Decision Flow for Optimality Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Candidate Point x star] --> B[Compute Gradient nabla f of x star]
    B --> C{Is nabla f of x star equals zero vector?}
    C -- No --> D[Not a Stationary Point - No Local Optimum]
    C -- Yes --> E[Stationary Point Confirmed - Proceed to Second-Order Test]
    E --> F[Compute Hessian Matrix H of x star]
    F --> G{Determine Definiteness of H}
    G -- Positive Definite --> H[STRICT LOCAL MINIMUM Confirmed]
    G -- Negative Definite --> I[STRICT LOCAL MAXIMUM Confirmed]
    G -- Positive Semi-Definite --> J[Possible Local Minimum - Higher Order Test Needed]
    G -- Negative Semi-Definite --> K[Possible Local Maximum - Higher Order Test Needed]
    G -- Indefinite --> L[SADDLE POINT Confirmed]
    G -- Zero Matrix --> M[Inconclusive - Use Higher Derivatives or Graphical Method]
    H --> N[Apply Convexity Test on Domain]
    I --> N
    J --> N
    K --> N
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O{Is f Convex on Entire Domain?}
    O -- Yes and Local Min --> P[Local Min is GLOBAL MIN]
    O -- Yes and Local Max --> Q[Local Max is GLOBAL MAX]
    O -- No --> R[Only Local Conclusion Valid - Global Search Required]
```

### 4.2 Convexity Verification Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Given Function f of x] --> B{Is f twice continuously differentiable?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Compute Hessian H of x]
    C --> D{Is H positive semi-definite for all x in domain?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Function is CONVEX]
    D -- No --> F{Is H negative semi-definite for all x in domain?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Function is CONCAVE]
    F -- No --> H[Function is NEITHER convex nor concave]
    B -- No --> I[Apply First-Order Definition Test]
    I --> J{Check f of lambda x plus one minus lambda y compared to lambda f x plus one minus lambda f y}
    J -- Holds --> E
    J -- Reversed --> G
    J -- Fails --> H
    E --> K[Apply Optimality Theorem: Local Min equals Global Min]
    G --> L[Apply Optimality Theorem: Local Max equals Global Max]
    H --> M[Use Numerical Methods: Gradient Descent, Newton Method]
```

### 4.3 Geometric Classification of Critical Points

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Critical Point Classification Logic] --> B[Compute Hessian Eigenvalues]
    B --> C{Number of Negative Eigenvalues}
    C -- Zero --> D1[Local MINIMUM - Bowl Shape]
    C -- One --> D2[Saddle Point - Pass Shape]
    C -- Two --> D3[Local MAXIMUM - Dome Shape]
    C -- All n --> D4[Global MAXIMUM on full domain]
    C -- All n zero --> D5[Flat Region - Check higher derivatives]
    D1 --> E[H positive definite - Strict local min]
    D2 --> F[H indefinite - Saddle confirmed]
    D3 --> G[H negative definite - Strict local max]
    D4 --> H[Strict concave - Global max]
    D5 --> I[Degenerate case - Use third-order Taylor]
```

### 4.4 Visual Schematic of Convex vs. Concave Functions

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph SC1[CONVEX FUNCTION EXAMPLE: f of x equals x squared]
        X1[Convex Bowl] --> X2[Global Minimum at x equals 0]
        X2 --> X3[All local minima equal global minimum]
    end
    subgraph SC2[CONCAVE FUNCTION EXAMPLE: f of x equals minus x squared]
        Y1[Concave Dome] --> Y2[Global Maximum at x equals 0]
        Y2 --> Y3[All local maxima equal global maximum]
    end
    subgraph SC3[NON-CONVEX FUNCTION EXAMPLE: f of x equals x cubed minus 3x]
        Z1[Wavy Curve] --> Z2[Local Max at x equals minus 1]
        Z2 --> Z3[Local Min at x equals plus 1]
        Z3 --> Z4[Saddle or inflection may occur]
    end
```
