topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT723 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Optimization Algorithms for Solving Constrained Optimization Problems - Direct Methods - Penalty Function Methods, Barrier Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Constrained Optimization Problem (COP)** seeks to find the vector $\mathbf{x}^* = (x_1^*, x_2^*, \ldots, x_n^*)$ that minimizes (or maximizes) an objective function subject to a set of constraints. In standard form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimize} \quad & f(\mathbf{x}) \\
\text{Subject to} \quad & g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, m \\
& h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, p
\end{aligned}
$$

where $f(\mathbf{x})$ is the **objective function**, $g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$ are **inequality constraints**, and $h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ are **equality constraints**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Direct methods solve constrained problems by *transforming* them into a sequence of unconstrained problems. They do **not** require the explicit computation of Lagrange multipliers (unlike indirect methods). The Penalty Function Method and the Barrier Method are the two pillars of this approach.

## 1.2 Classification of Direct Methods

Direct (or *transformational*) methods replace the original constrained problem with an **augmented function** $P(\mathbf{x}, \mu_k)$ that penalizes constraint violation. The parameter $\mu_k$ is gradually driven to a limiting value so the sequence of unconstrained optima converges to the true constrained optimum.

| Method | Penalty Direction | Driving Limit | Constraint Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Exterior Penalty Method** | Outside feasible region | $\mu_k \to \infty$ | Inequality + Equality |
| **Interior Penalty (Barrier) Method** | Inside feasible region | $\mu_k \to 0$ | Inequality only |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — The "Hiking with Boundaries" Picture:**
> Imagine you are hiking down a mountain to find the lowest valley (the optimum). However, you are fenced in — the fence is the **constraint**.
> - **Exterior Penalty Method (Fence with Stinging Nettles):** You can step *through* the fence, but each time you do, you get stung with a penalty proportional to how far past the fence you go. As the nettles get worse (penalty parameter $R \to \infty$), you learn to stay strictly inside. You may start *outside* the fence.
> - **Interior Barrier Method (Invisible Bouncy Wall):** You start *inside* the safe zone. As you approach the fence, an invisible, increasingly stiff cushion pushes you back. The cushion stiffness is controlled by a parameter $r \to 0$, so the wall becomes softer, letting you settle *right at the boundary* where the true optimum lies. You can never cross the fence.

## 1.4 Why Use Direct Methods?

- They convert a **hard constrained problem** into a sequence of **easier unconstrained problems** that can be solved by standard gradient methods (Newton, Quasi-Newton, Fletcher-Reeves).
- They handle **non-differentiable**, **non-convex**, or **black-box** constraints.
- They avoid the numerical difficulties of solving the **Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT)** system directly.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** In KTU 2024 paper-style questions, the *algorithmic step-by-step* solution of a small numerical problem (e.g., 1-D or 2-D) using penalty/barrier method is asked for **7-14 marks**. Memorize the augmented function form and the update rule for the penalty parameter.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The General Penalty Framework

The constrained problem is replaced by an unconstrained minimization of the **augmented objective function**:

$$
P(\mathbf{x}, \mu_k) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \mu_k \cdot \Phi(\mathbf{x})
$$

where:
- $f(\mathbf{x})$ = original objective function
- $\Phi(\mathbf{x})$ = **penalty function** that becomes large when $\mathbf{x}$ violates constraints
- $\mu_k$ = **penalty parameter** (positive scalar that changes with iteration $k$)
- $P(\mathbf{x}, \mu_k)$ is also called the **composite function** or **merit function**

The sequence $\{\mathbf{x}_k^*\}$ of unconstrained minima of $P(\mathbf{x}, \mu_k)$ is forced to converge to $\mathbf{x}^*$ (the true constrained optimum) as $\mu_k$ is updated.

## 2.2 Exterior Penalty Function Method (Quadratic Loss Method)

### 2.2.1 Construction

For inequality constraints $g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$, the **quadratic exterior penalty** is:

$$
\Phi(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ \max \left( 0, \; g_i(\mathbf{x}) \right) \right]^{2}
$$

The augmented function becomes:

$$
P(\mathbf{x}, \mu_k) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \mu_k \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ \max \left( 0, \; g_i(\mathbf{x}) \right) \right]^{2}
$$

> The $\max(0, g_i)$ term ensures only *violated* constraints are penalized. Feasible points pay **zero** penalty.

For equality constraints $h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$:

$$
\Phi_h(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{j=1}^{p} \left[ h_j(\mathbf{x}) \right]^{2}
$$

### 2.2.2 Algorithm Steps

1. Choose initial point $\mathbf{x}_0$ (can be **infeasible**), initial penalty $\mu_1 > 0$ (e.g., $\mu_1 = 1$), and a tolerance $\epsilon > 0$. Set counter $k = 1$.
2. Form the augmented function $P(\mathbf{x}, \mu_k)$.
3. Find the unconstrained minimum $\mathbf{x}_k^* = \arg\min_{\mathbf{x}} P(\mathbf{x}, \mu_k)$ using gradient-based methods.
4. **Convergence Check:** If $\mu_k \cdot \Phi(\mathbf{x}_k^*) \le \epsilon$, stop and report $\mathbf{x}_k^*$ as the solution.
5. Otherwise, increase the penalty parameter: $\mu_{k+1} = c \cdot \mu_k$, where $c > 1$ (commonly $c = 5$ or $c = 10$).
6. Set $\mathbf{x}_{k+1}^{(0)} = \mathbf{x}_k^*$ (warm start) and return to Step 2 with $k = k+1$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limitation of the Quadratic Penalty Method:** As $\mu_k \to \infty$, the Hessian of $P$ becomes ill-conditioned (the contour lines of $\Phi$ become highly eccentric), causing numerical instability in Newton-type solvers. This is a common viva question.

## 2.3 Interior Penalty (Barrier) Method

### 2.3.1 Motivation

Used when the feasible region has a non-empty interior and the optimum lies on the **boundary**. The penalty function $B(\mathbf{x})$ becomes **infinite** at the constraint boundary, blocking the path outward.

### 2.3.2 Common Barrier Functions

**Type 1 — Inverse Barrier Function (Reciprocal):**

$$
B(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \frac{1}{-g_i(\mathbf{x})}
$$

Augmented function:

$$
P(\mathbf{x}, r_k) = f(\mathbf{x}) + r_k \sum_{i=1}^{m} \frac{1}{-g_i(\mathbf{x})}
$$

**Type 2 — Logarithmic Barrier Function (More Popular):**

$$
B(\mathbf{x}) = - \sum_{i=1}^{m} \ln \left[ -g_i(\mathbf{x}) \right]
$$

Augmented function:

$$
P(\mathbf{x}, r_k) = f(\mathbf{x}) - r_k \sum_{i=1}^{m} \ln \left[ -g_i(\mathbf{x}) \right]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why logarithmic?** It is smooth, convex (for convex constraints), and its gradient is well-behaved. The logarithmic barrier is the foundation of modern **Interior Point Methods (IPM)** used in linear programming (Karmarkar's algorithm).

### 2.3.3 Algorithm Steps

1. Choose a strictly **interior feasible** initial point $\mathbf{x}_0$ (must satisfy $g_i(\mathbf{x}_0) < 0$ for all $i$). Set initial barrier parameter $r_1 > 0$ and tolerance $\epsilon > 0$. Set $k = 1$.
2. Form the augmented function $P(\mathbf{x}, r_k)$.
3. Find the unconstrained minimum $\mathbf{x}_k^* = \arg\min_{\mathbf{x}} P(\mathbf{x}, r_k)$.
4. **Convergence Check:** If $r_k \cdot B(\mathbf{x}_k^*) \le \epsilon$, stop and report $\mathbf{x}_k^*$.
5. Otherwise, **decrease** the barrier parameter: $r_{k+1} = r_k / c$, where $c > 1$.
6. Set $\mathbf{x}_{k+1}^{(0)} = \mathbf{x}_k^*$ and return to Step 2 with $k = k+1$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Save this table — these formulas appear directly in KTU 2024 ESE questions.

| # | Method | Augmented Function $P(\mathbf{x}, \mu)$ | Penalty Term | Parameter Update |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Quadratic Exterior (Loss)** | $f(\mathbf{x}) + \mu \sum \left[ \max(0, g_i) \right]^{2}$ | $\sum \left[ \max(0, g_i) \right]^{2}$ | $\mu_{k+1} = c \mu_k$, $c > 1$, $\mu_k \uparrow$ |
| 2 | **Quadratic Exterior (Equality)** | $f(\mathbf{x}) + \mu \sum \left[ h_j \right]^{2}$ | $\sum h_j^{2}$ | $\mu_{k+1} = c \mu_k$ |
| 3 | **Inverse Barrier (Interior)** | $f(\mathbf{x}) + r \sum \dfrac{1}{-g_i(\mathbf{x})}$ | $\sum \dfrac{1}{-g_i}$ | $r_{k+1} = r_k / c$, $r_k \downarrow 0$ |
| 4 | **Logarithmic Barrier (Interior)** | $f(\mathbf{x}) - r \sum \ln\left[ -g_i(\mathbf{x}) \right]$ | $- \sum \ln(-g_i)$ | $r_{k+1} = r_k / c$ |
| 5 | **General Convergence Criterion** | $\mu_k \cdot \Phi(\mathbf{x}_k^*) \le \epsilon$ | — | $\epsilon \approx 10^{-4}$ to $10^{-6}$ |

## 2.5 Comparison: Exterior vs. Interior

| Property | Exterior Penalty | Interior Barrier |
|---|---|---|
| **Starting Point** | Can be infeasible | Must be **strictly interior** |
| **Parameter Limit** | $\mu \to \infty$ | $r \to 0$ |
| **Constraint Type** | Inequality + Equality | Inequality only (with strict feasible interior) |
| **Hessian Behaviour** | Ill-conditioned as $\mu \to \infty$ | Well-conditioned throughout |
| **Modern Use** | Less common in practice | Foundation of Interior-Point Methods |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Structural Optimization** — Sizing of trusses with stress constraints.
- **Process Engineering** — Chemical reactor design with yield/safety constraints.
- **Aerospace Trajectory Optimization** — Fuel-optimal paths with mission constraints.
- **Portfolio Management** — Risk-return optimization with budget and sector caps.
- **Power Systems** — Economic dispatch with generator capacity and voltage limits.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Quadratic Exterior Penalty (1-D Problem)

**Problem (KTU 2024 Pattern):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimize} \quad & f(x) = (x - 2)^{2} \\
\text{Subject to} \quad & g(x) = x - 1 \le 0 \\
& x \ge 0
\end{aligned}
$$

The unconstrained minimum of $f(x)$ occurs at $x = 2$, but the constraint forces the optimum to the boundary $x^* = 1$, giving $f^* = 1$.

### Step 1: Form the Penalty Function

Since the constraint is $g(x) = x - 1 \le 0$, the violated region is $x > 1$. The penalty term is $[\max(0, x-1)]^2$.

$$
P(x, \mu_k) = (x - 2)^{2} + \mu_k \left[ \max(0, x - 1) \right]^{2}
$$

### Step 2: Solve the Unconstrained Sub-Problem (Region $x > 1$)

For $x > 1$, $\max(0, x-1) = x - 1$. So:

$$
P(x, \mu_k) = (x - 2)^{2} + \mu_k (x - 1)^{2}
$$

### Step 3: Differentiate and Set to Zero

$$
\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} = 2(x - 2) + 2 \mu_k (x - 1) = 0
$$

Divide by 2:

$$
x - 2 + \mu_k (x - 1) = 0
$$

### Step 4: Solve for $x_k^*$

$$
x(1 + \mu_k) = 2 + \mu_k
$$

$$
\boxed{x_k^* = \frac{2 + \mu_k}{1 + \mu_k}}
$$

### Step 5: Iterate with $\mu_{k+1} = 10 \mu_k$, Starting $\mu_1 = 1$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_1 = 1 \;&\Rightarrow\; x_1^* = \frac{3}{2} = 1.5000,\quad f(x_1^*) = 0.2500,\quad \mu_1 \Phi = 1 \cdot (0.5)^2 = 0.2500 \\
\mu_2 = 10 \;&\Rightarrow\; x_2^* = \frac{12}{11} = 1.0909,\quad f(x_2^*) = 0.8264,\quad \mu_2 \Phi = 10 \cdot (0.0909)^2 = 0.0826 \\
\mu_3 = 100 \;&\Rightarrow\; x_3^* = \frac{102}{101} = 1.0099,\quad f(x_3^*) = 0.9803,\quad \mu_3 \Phi = 100 \cdot (0.0099)^2 = 0.0098 \\
\mu_4 = 1000 \;&\Rightarrow\; x_4^* = \frac{1002}{1001} = 1.00099,\quad f(x_4^*) = 0.9980
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification of Convergence:** As $\mu_k \to \infty$, $x_k^* \to 1$ (the true optimum). ✓

> [!NOTE]
> The penalty term $\mu_k \Phi$ shrinks toward zero, satisfying the convergence criterion $\mu_k \cdot \Phi(x_k^*) \le \epsilon$ for $\epsilon = 0.01$ at $k = 3$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Logarithmic Barrier (2-D Problem)

**Problem:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimize} \quad & f(\mathbf{x}) = x_1^{2} + x_2^{2} \\
\text{Subject to} \quad & g(\mathbf{x}) = 2 - x_1 - x_2 \le 0
\end{aligned}
$$

The constraint says $x_1 + x_2 \ge 2$. By symmetry, the optimum lies at $x_1^* = x_2^* = 1$, with $f^* = 2$.

### Step 1: Form the Logarithmic Barrier Function

$$
P(\mathbf{x}, r_k) = x_1^{2} + x_2^{2} - r_k \ln\left[ -(2 - x_1 - x_2) \right] = x_1^{2} + x_2^{2} - r_k \ln(x_1 + x_2 - 2)
$$

### Step 2: Set Up Stationarity Conditions

Let $s = x_1 + x_2 - 2$. The first-order conditions are:

$$
\frac{\partial P}{\partial x_1} = 2 x_1 - \frac{r_k}{x_1 + x_2 - 2} = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial P}{\partial x_2} = 2 x_2 - \frac{r_k}{x_1 + x_2 - 2} = 0
$$

### Step 3: Solve by Symmetry

Subtracting the two equations:

$$
2(x_1 - x_2) = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; x_1 = x_2
$$

### Step 4: Substitute Back

Let $x_1 = x_2 = x$. Then $x_1 + x_2 - 2 = 2x - 2 = 2(x - 1)$.

$$
2x - \frac{r_k}{2(x - 1)} = 0
$$

Multiply by $2(x-1)$:

$$
4x(x - 1) - r_k = 0
$$

$$
4x^{2} - 4x - r_k = 0
$$

### Step 5: Solve the Quadratic for $x$

$$
x = \frac{4 \pm \sqrt{16 + 16 r_k}}{8} = \frac{1 \pm \sqrt{1 + r_k}}{2}
$$

Since we need $x > 1$ (interior of feasible region), we take the **positive** root:

$$
\boxed{x_k^* = \frac{1 + \sqrt{1 + r_k}}{2}}
$$

### Step 6: Iterate with $r_{k+1} = r_k / 10$, Starting $r_1 = 1$

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_1 = 1 \;&\Rightarrow\; x_1^* = \frac{1 + \sqrt{2}}{2} = 1.2071,\quad f_1 = 2(1.2071)^2 = 2.9142 \\
r_2 = 0.1 \;&\Rightarrow\; x_2^* = \frac{1 + \sqrt{1.1}}{2} = 1.0244,\quad f_2 = 2(1.0244)^2 = 2.0988 \\
r_3 = 0.01 \;&\Rightarrow\; x_3^* = \frac{1 + \sqrt{1.01}}{2} = 1.00249,\quad f_3 = 2(1.00249)^2 = 2.00498 \\
r_4 = 0.001 \;&\Rightarrow\; x_4^* \approx 1.00025,\quad f_4 \approx 2.0005
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** As $r_k \to 0$, $x_k^* \to 1$ and $f_k \to 2$. ✓

> [!TIP]
> **Pattern Recognition for KTU:** When the problem is symmetric, exploit $x_1 = x_2$ to reduce it to a single variable. This makes derivatives and algebra dramatically easier on the exam.

---

## 3.3 General Convergence Theorem (Kelley's Theorem)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (Kelley, 1960):** Let $\{\mu_k\}$ be a monotonically increasing sequence with $\mu_k \to \infty$. Let $\mathbf{x}_k^* = \arg\min_{\mathbf{x}} P(\mathbf{x}, \mu_k)$ for the exterior quadratic penalty. If $f$ and $\Phi$ are continuous and a unique constrained optimum $\mathbf{x}^*$ exists, then:
> $$\lim_{k \to \infty} \mathbf{x}_k^* = \mathbf{x}^*$$
> Furthermore, if $P(\cdot, \mu_k)$ is strictly convex for each $k$, convergence is **quadratic** near the optimum.

The analogous result for the barrier method with $r_k \to 0$ is called the **Barrier Method Convergence Theorem** and is typically proved in KTU Module 3 tutorial sheets.

---

## 3.4 Self-Assessment Mini-Derivation

**Try Yourself:** Show that for the problem

$$
\text{Minimize } f(x) = x^{2}, \quad \text{subject to } x \ge 1
$$

the logarithmic barrier solution is:

$$
x_k^* = 1 + \sqrt{r_k}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Solution Hint:** $P(x, r_k) = x^{2} - r_k \ln(x - 1)$. Set $\frac{dP}{dx} = 2x - \frac{r_k}{x - 1} = 0$ and solve.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Penalty Method Flowchart (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the complete algorithmic flow of the **Exterior Quadratic Penalty Method** as it would be implemented in a KTU Module 3 question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([START]) --> B[Initialize x0, mu1 = 1, eps, k = 1]
    B --> C[Form Augmented Function P x, mu_k]
    C --> D[Solve unconstrained min of P using gradient methods]
    D --> E[Obtain xk star]
    E --> F[Compute penalty value mu_k times Phi of xk star]
    F --> G{mu_k Phi xk star less than eps?}
    G -- YES --> H([Report xk star as solution])
    G -- NO --> I[Update mu k plus 1 = c times mu_k, c greater than 1]
    I --> J[Set warm start x k plus 1 = xk star]
    J --> K[Increment k = k plus 1]
    K --> C
```

## 4.2 Barrier Method Flowchart (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([START]) --> B[Choose strictly interior x0 with gi of x0 less than 0]
    B --> C[Set r1 greater than 0, eps, k = 1]
    C --> D[Form Barrier P x, rk = f x minus rk sum ln of negative gi x]
    D --> E[Solve unconstrained min of P via Newton or Quasi Newton]
    E --> F[Obtain xk star]
    F --> G[Compute rk times B of xk star]
    G --> H{rk B of xk star less than eps?}
    H -- YES --> I([Report xk star as solution])
    H -- NO --> J[Update r k plus 1 = rk divided by c, c greater than 1]
    J --> K[Set warm start x k plus 1 = xk star]
    K --> L[Increment k = k plus 1]
    L --> D
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Block Diagram — Exterior vs. Interior Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Constrained Problem] --> B{Type of feasible region}
    B -- Has interior, optimum on boundary --> C[Use Barrier Method]
    B -- Optimum inside or no strict interior --> D[Use Exterior Penalty]
    C --> C1[Logarithmic or Inverse Barrier]
    C --> C2[Start strictly interior]
    C --> C3[Drive r to 0]
    D --> D1[Quadratic Loss Function]
    D --> D2[Start anywhere, even infeasible]
    D --> D3[Drive mu to infinity]
    C1 --> E[Sequence of Unconstrained Minima]
    C2 --> E
    C3 --> E
    D1 --> E
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F([Converges to Constrained Optimum])
```

## 4.4 Penalty Mechanism Visualization (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph F[Feasible Region g of x less than 0]
    F1[Interior x strictly feasible]
    end
    subgraph I[Infeasible Region g of x greater than 0]
    I1[Violation zone]
    end
    F1 -- "Barrier pushes back as x approaches boundary" --> Boundary
    Boundary -- "Infeasible solutions are penalized" --> I1
    Boundary([Constraint boundary g of x = 0])
```

> [!NOTE]
> The schematics above map the **functional architecture** of penalty/barrier algorithms. They replace a static 2-D geometric diagram (which Mermaid cannot draw) with a clear logical flow of how the algorithm interacts with the feasible region.

## 4.5 Penalty Function Behaviour Plot (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Penalty Parameter mu increases] --> B[Quadratic Loss Curve Steepens]
    B --> C[Solution xk star moves toward constraint boundary]
    C --> D[Constraint violation mu times Phi of xk star decreases]
    D --> E([Convergence to true optimum x star])
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between interior penalty function method and exterior penalty function method. State any two advantages of the barrier method.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Exterior Penalty | Interior Barrier |
|---|---|---|
| **Starting Point** | Can be infeasible | Must be strictly interior |
| **Parameter Limit** | $\mu \to \infty$ | $r \to 0$ |
| **Constraint Type** | Inequality + Equality | Inequality only |

**Two advantages of Barrier Method:**
1. The Hessian of the augmented function remains well-conditioned, so Newton-type solvers are numerically stable.
2. It naturally generates primal-feasible iterates, which is useful in real-time engineering applications.

---

### Question A.2 — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**State the logarithmic barrier function for a constrained optimization problem with $m$ inequality constraints. Why is the starting point required to be strictly feasible?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The logarithmic barrier function is:

$$
B(\mathbf{x}) = - \sum_{i=1}^{m} \ln \left[ - g_i(\mathbf{x}) \right]
$$

Augmented function:

$$
P(\mathbf{x}, r_k) = f(\mathbf{x}) - r_k \sum_{i=1}^{m} \ln \left[ - g_i(\mathbf{x}) \right]
$$

**Why strictly feasible?** Because the term $\ln[-g_i(\mathbf{x})]$ is **undefined** when $g_i(\mathbf{x}) \ge 0$. For the augmented function to be finite and differentiable, the starting point must satisfy $g_i(\mathbf{x}_0) < 0$ for all $i$ (1 mark for the definition, 2 marks for the explanation).

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

### [KTU University Exam - Model Question]

**Question:** Solve the following constrained optimization problem using the **Quadratic Exterior Penalty Method** for two iterations starting with $\mu_1 = 1$ and updating $\mu_{k+1} = 10 \mu_k$:

$$
\text{Minimize } f(x) = (x - 3)^{2}, \quad \text{subject to } g(x) = x - 2 \le 0
$$

Use the initial guess $x_0 = 4$ (infeasible). Take $\epsilon = 0.1$.

### Solution (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — Form the Penalty Function (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Identify the violated region. The constraint $x - 2 \le 0$ is violated when $x > 2$.

**Step 2:** Form the augmented function:

$$
P(x, \mu_k) = (x - 3)^{2} + \mu_k \left[ \max(0, x - 2) \right]^{2}
$$

For $x > 2$ (the region where the penalty is active):

$$
P(x, \mu_k) = (x - 3)^{2} + \mu_k (x - 2)^{2}
$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate with respect to $x$:

$$
\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} = 2(x - 3) + 2 \mu_k (x - 2) = 0
$$

Divide by 2:

$$
x - 3 + \mu_k (x - 2) = 0
$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $x_k^*$:

$$
x(1 + \mu_k) = 3 + 2 \mu_k
$$

$$
x_k^* = \frac{3 + 2 \mu_k}{1 + \mu_k}
$$

#### Part (b) — Perform Two Iterations (7 marks)

**Iteration 1: $\mu_1 = 1$**

$$
x_1^* = \frac{3 + 2(1)}{1 + 1} = \frac{5}{2} = 2.5
$$

Check feasibility: $x_1^* = 2.5 > 2$ (infeasible — penalty still active). Compute penalty value:

$$
\mu_1 \cdot \Phi(x_1^*) = 1 \cdot (2.5 - 2)^{2} = 0.25
$$

Since $0.25 > 0.1$, do not stop.

**Iteration 2: $\mu_2 = 10$**

$$
x_2^* = \frac{3 + 2(10)}{1 + 10} = \frac{23}{11} \approx 2.0909
$$

Check feasibility: $x_2^* = 2.0909 > 2$ (infeasible). Compute penalty value:

$$
\mu_2 \cdot \Phi(x_2^*) = 10 \cdot (2.0909 - 2)^{2} = 10 \cdot (0.0909)^{2} = 10 \cdot 0.00826 = 0.0826
$$

Since $0.0826 < 0.1$, **STOP**. Report $x_2^* \approx 2.0909$ as the approximate solution.

**Verification:** The true constrained optimum is $x^* = 2$ (on the boundary), and the algorithm converged close to it. ✓

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

### [KTU University Exam - Model Question]

**Question:** Solve the following constrained optimization problem using the **Logarithmic Barrier Method** for two iterations starting with $r_1 = 0.5$ and updating $r_{k+1} = r_k / 10$:

$$
\text{Minimize } f(x_1, x_2) = (x_1 - 1)^{2} + (x_2 - 1)^{2}, \quad \text{subject to } g(\mathbf{x}) = 2 - x_1 - x_2 \le 0
$$

Take the initial strictly feasible point $\mathbf{x}_0 = (1.5, 1.5)^{T}$ and tolerance $\epsilon = 0.05$.

### Solution (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — Form the Barrier Function and Stationarity Equations (7 marks)

**Step 1:** The constraint $-x_1 - x_2 + 2 \le 0$ is equivalent to $x_1 + x_2 \ge 2$. The interior is $x_1 + x_2 > 2$.

**Step 2:** Form the augmented function:

$$
P(\mathbf{x}, r_k) = (x_1 - 1)^{2} + (x_2 - 1)^{2} - r_k \ln(x_1 + x_2 - 2)
$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate:

$$
\frac{\partial P}{\partial x_1} = 2(x_1 - 1) - \frac{r_k}{x_1 + x_2 - 2} = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial P}{\partial x_2} = 2(x_2 - 1) - \frac{r_k}{x_1 + x_2 - 2} = 0
$$

**Step 4:** Subtract the two equations:

$$
2(x_1 - x_2) = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; x_1 = x_2
$$

By symmetry, $x_1 = x_2 = x$. Substituting:

$$
2(x - 1) - \frac{r_k}{2x - 2} = 0
$$

Multiply both sides by $2(x - 1)$:

$$
4(x - 1)^{2} - r_k = 0
$$

$$
(x - 1)^{2} = \frac{r_k}{4}
$$

$$
x = 1 + \frac{\sqrt{r_k}}{2} \quad (\text{taking positive root for interior point})
$$

#### Part (b) — Perform Two Iterations (7 marks)

**Iteration 1: $r_1 = 0.5$**

$$
x_1 = 1 + \frac{\sqrt{0.5}}{2} = 1 + \frac{0.7071}{2} = 1.3536
$$

So $\mathbf{x}_1^* = (1.3536, 1.3536)^{T}$. Compute $f_1$:

$$
f_1 = 2 \times (0.3536)^{2} = 0.2500
$$

Compute penalty value $r_1 B(\mathbf{x}_1^*) = 0.5 \cdot [-\ln(2 \times 1.3536 - 2)] = -0.5 \ln(0.7071) = -0.5 \times (-0.3466) = 0.1733$

Since $0.1733 > 0.05$, continue.

**Iteration 2: $r_2 = 0.05$**

$$
x_2 = 1 + \frac{\sqrt{0.05}}{2} = 1 + \frac{0.2236}{2} = 1.1118
$$

So $\mathbf{x}_2^* = (1.1118, 1.1118)^{T}$. Compute $f_2$:

$$
f_2 = 2 \times (0.1118)^{2} = 0.0250
$$

Compute penalty value $r_2 B(\mathbf{x}_2^*) = 0.05 \cdot [-\ln(2 \times 1.1118 - 2)] = -0.05 \ln(0.2236) = -0.05 \times (-1.4979) = 0.0749$

Since $0.0749 > 0.05$, continue.

(Optional: at $r_3 = 0.005$, $x_3 = 1.0354$, penalty $\approx 0.0237 < 0.05$. STOP.)

**Verification:** The true constrained optimum is $\mathbf{x}^* = (1, 1)^{T}$ with $f^* = 0$. The barrier method drives the iterates toward this point as $r_k \to 0$. ✓

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Penalty/Barrier Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\max(0, g_i)$ in the exterior penalty** (loses 1-2 marks). Only *violated* constraints should be penalized; the penalty is zero inside the feasible region.
> 2. **Choosing an infeasible starting point for the barrier method** — the question often tests this explicitly. You will lose **2 marks** for not justifying the strict-feasibility requirement.
> 3. **Wrong parameter update direction** — exterior penalty uses $\mu \to \infty$ (multiply by $c > 1$); barrier uses $r \to 0$ (divide by $c > 1$ or multiply by factor $< 1$). Mixing these up is a frequent error.
> 4. **Not verifying stationarity with $\partial P / \partial x = 0$** — you must show the derivative step explicitly. Skipping the derivative loses 2-3 marks.
> 5. **Convergence criterion confusion** — the correct check is $\mu_k \cdot \Phi(\mathbf{x}_k^*) \le \epsilon$ (or $r_k \cdot B \le \epsilon$), **not** just the difference in $f$ values.
> 6. **Algebraic slip in the update formula** — for $x_k^* = (2 + \mu_k)/(1 + \mu_k)$, students often write $x_k^* = (2 \mu_k + 1)/(\mu_k + 1)$ which is algebraically correct, but introducing a sign error or mis-simplification costs 1 mark.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision Points for Module 3:**

- **Penalty Function Methods** convert a constrained problem to a sequence of unconstrained problems via an *augmented function* $P(\mathbf{x}, \mu) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \mu \cdot \Phi(\mathbf{x})$.
- **Exterior Penalty** (Quadratic Loss): $\Phi = \sum [\max(0, g_i)]^2 + \sum h_j^2$; $\mu_k \to \infty$; starting point can be infeasible.
- **Interior Barrier** (Logarithmic or Inverse): uses $- \sum \ln(-g_i)$ or $\sum 1/(-g_i)$; $r_k \to 0$; starting point must be strictly interior.
- **Logarithmic barrier** is the modern choice (used in **Interior Point Methods** for LP/NLP).
- **Update rules:** $\mu_{k+1} = c \mu_k$ for exterior; $r_{k+1} = r_k / c$ for interior (with $c = 5$ or $10$).
- **Convergence criterion:** $\mu_k \cdot \Phi(\mathbf{x}_k^*) \le \epsilon$ (commonly $\epsilon = 10^{-4}$).
- **Algorithm pattern:** Initialize $\to$ Form $P$ $\to$ Solve unconstrained min $\to$ Check $\mu_k \Phi \le \epsilon$ $\to$ Update $\mu$ and iterate.
- **Limitation of exterior penalty:** Hessian becomes ill-conditioned as $\mu \to \infty$.
- **Limitation of interior barrier:** Cannot handle equality constraints directly (must be converted to two inequalities).
- **Kelley's Theorem** guarantees convergence to the true constrained optimum under mild regularity conditions.
- **Numerical example standard form:** 1-D problems like $\min (x-a)^2$ s.t. $x \le b$ and 2-D symmetric problems like $\min x_1^2 + x_2^2$ s.t. $x_1 + x_2 \ge c$ are the most-asked patterns in KTU 2024 papers.
- **Always** exploit symmetry ($x_1 = x_2$) to reduce a 2-D problem to 1-D in exam solutions — it shows maturity and saves time.
- **Final convergence answer** for a problem of the form $\min (x-a)^2$ s.t. $x \le b$ is always $x^* = b$ (active boundary), and the answer for $\min x_1^2 + x_2^2$ s.t. $x_1 + x_2 \ge c$ is always $x_1^* = x_2^* = c/2$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optimization of Function of Multiple Variables Subject to Equality Constraints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT723 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Optimization of Function of Multiple Variables Subject to Equality Constraints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

A **Constrained Optimization Problem with Equality Constraints** is mathematically formulated as:

> **Minimize (or Maximize)** $f(\mathbf{x}) = f(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$
> **Subject to** $g_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, m$

where:
- $f: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$ is the **objective function** (scalar-valued).
- $g_j: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$ are the **equality constraints** (constraint functions).
- $m < n$ is a standard feasibility condition (number of constraints must be strictly less than the number of decision variables).
- $\mathbf{x}^* \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the **optimal feasible point**, and $\lambda_j$ are the **Lagrange multipliers** corresponding to each constraint.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Emphasis (2024 Scheme):** The phrase *"function of multiple variables"* explicitly signals $n \geq 2$, with equality (not inequality) constraints. The classical tool to solve such problems is the **Lagrange Multiplier Method** (Joseph-Louis Lagrange, 1788).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Climber on a Ridge"

Imagine a hiker standing on a foggy mountain (the *objective function* $f$) who wants to reach the **lowest valley** (minimum of $f$). However, the hiker is **not free to walk anywhere** — they are tied to a fixed ridge path (the *equality constraint* $g(\mathbf{x}) = 0$, e.g., a contour line on a map).

- The hiker **cannot descend straight down** the gradient $-\nabla f$ (that would leave the ridge).
- They must walk **along the ridge** while looking for the spot where the path is tangent to the level curves of $f$.
- **Intuitively**: At the optimal point, the slope of the ridge must match the slope of the mountain at that point — meaning the gradient of the constraint $\nabla g$ is **parallel** to the gradient of the objective $\nabla f$.

This parallelism is precisely the **Lagrange condition**: $\nabla f = \lambda \nabla g$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Multiple Variables" matters:** With $n \geq 2$, we work in a multi-dimensional space $\mathbb{R}^n$. The constraint $g(\mathbf{x})=0$ defines an $(n-1)$-dimensional *constraint surface* (a hyper-curve/hyper-surface). The optimum is found where $f$'s level set is **tangent** to this surface.

### 1.3 Classification of the Constrained Optimum

| Type | Description | Sign Convention |
|---|---|---|
| **Local Minimum** $\mathbf{x}^*$ | $f(\mathbf{x}^*) \leq f(\mathbf{x})$ for all $\mathbf{x}$ near $\mathbf{x}^*$ on the constraint surface | $\mathbf{x}^*$ is a "valley" |
| **Local Maximum** $\mathbf{x}^*$ | $f(\mathbf{x}^*) \geq f(\mathbf{x})$ for all $\mathbf{x}$ near $\mathbf{x}^*$ on the constraint surface | $\mathbf{x}^*$ is a "peak" |
| **Saddle Point** | Neither min nor max (only occurs when $m \geq 2$) | Curvature is mixed |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Lagrangian Function — Construction

For a problem with $n$ variables and $m$ equality constraints, the **augmented Lagrangian (or Lagrangian function)** is defined as:

$$L(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}) = f(\mathbf{x}) - \sum_{j=1}^{m} \lambda_j \, g_j(\mathbf{x})$$

or equivalently:

$$L(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{j=1}^{m} \lambda_j \, g_j(\mathbf{x})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention in KTU Textbooks:** The minus sign is more common in *minimization* literature; the plus sign appears in some maximization references. The choice of sign does **not** affect the location of the optimum — only the *sign* of $\lambda$ reverses. We adopt $L = f - \sum \lambda_j g_j$ as the **standard KTU convention**.

Here, $\boldsymbol{\lambda} = (\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_m)^T$ is the vector of **Lagrange multipliers** (also called *dual variables* or *shadow prices* in economics).

### 2.2 First-Order Necessary Condition (FONC) — The Lagrange Condition

> **Theorem (Lagrange, 1788):** Let $\mathbf{x}^*$ be a local extremum of $f$ subject to $g_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$, $j = 1,\ldots,m$. If $f$ and $g_j$ are continuously differentiable ($\mathcal{C}^1$), and the **constraint qualification** holds (i.e., the gradients $\nabla g_1, \ldots, \nabla g_m$ are linearly independent at $\mathbf{x}^*$), then there exist **unique** scalars $\lambda_1^*, \ldots, \lambda_m^*$ such that:

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} L(\mathbf{x}^*, \boldsymbol{\lambda}^*) = \mathbf{0} \quad \text{and} \quad \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\lambda}} L(\mathbf{x}^*, \boldsymbol{\lambda}^*) = \mathbf{0}$$

Component-wise, this yields the **system of $(n + m)$ equations in $(n + m)$ unknowns**:

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_i} - \sum_{j=1}^{m} \lambda_j \frac{\partial g_j}{\partial x_i} = 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

$$g_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, m$$

### 2.3 Second-Order Conditions — The Bordered Hessian

The Bordered Hessian $\mathbf{H}_B$ is an $(n+m) \times (n+m)$ symmetric matrix used to classify the critical point:

$$\mathbf{H}_B = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{0}_{m \times m} & \nabla g^T \\ \nabla g & \mathbf{H}_f \end{bmatrix}$$

where:
- $\mathbf{0}_{m \times m}$ is the $m \times m$ zero matrix.
- $\nabla g$ is the $m \times n$ Jacobian matrix of constraints, with entries $(\nabla g)_{ji} = \dfrac{\partial g_j}{\partial x_i}$.
- $\mathbf{H}_f$ is the $n \times n$ Hessian of $f$, with entries $(H_f)_{ik} = \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_i \partial x_k}$.

**Eliminating the constraint subspace**, the relevant test involves the **last $(n - m)$ leading principal minors** of the *reduced bordered Hessian*, i.e., the $(n+m-q) \times (n+m-q)$ determinant where $q$ ranges from $1$ to $n-m$.

#### Sign Rule Table (KTU Board Favorite):

| Type of Optimum | Sign of $\vert \mathbf{H}_B^{(n+m-q)} \vert$ for $q = 1, 2, \ldots, n-m$ |
|---|---|
| **Local Minimum** | Sign must be $(-1)^m$ |
| **Local Maximum** | Sign must alternate as $(-1)^{n-q}$ for $q = 1, \ldots, n-m$ |
| **Saddle Point** | No consistent sign pattern |

For the special case of **one equality constraint** ($m = 1$, $n$ variables), the test reduces to examining the **Hessian of the Lagrangian** restricted to the constraint surface.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Condition | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Lagrangian Function** | $L(\mathbf{x},\boldsymbol{\lambda}) = f(\mathbf{x}) - \sum_{j=1}^{m} \lambda_j g_j(\mathbf{x})$ | Augments $f$ with weighted constraints |
| 2 | **Stationarity (FONC)** | $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial x_i} = 0 \; \forall i$ | Gives $n$ equations |
| 3 | **Feasibility (Primal)** | $g_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0 \; \forall j$ | Gives $m$ equations |
| 4 | **Hessian of $L$** | $\nabla^2_{xx} L = \mathbf{H}_f - \sum_{j=1}^{m} \lambda_j \mathbf{H}_{g_j}$ | Used in second-order test |
| 5 | **Bordered Hessian** | $\mathbf{H}_B = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{0} & \nabla g^T \\ \nabla g & \mathbf{H}_f \end{bmatrix}$ | $(n+m) \times (n+m)$ matrix |
| 6 | **Min Test ($m=1$)** | Last $n-1$ principal minors have sign $(-1)^1 = -$ | Negative |
| 7 | **Max Test ($m=1$)** | Last $n-1$ principal minors alternate: $(-1)^{n-q}$ for $q=1,\ldots,n-1$ | Pattern depends on parity |
| 8 | **Constraint Qualification** | $\text{rank}(\nabla g) = m$ at optimum | Ensures multipliers exist |
| 9 | **Economic Interpretation** | $\lambda_j^* = -\dfrac{\partial f^*}{\partial b_j}$ where $b_j$ perturbs $g_j$ | "Shadow price" |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** The constraint qualification (gradients of $g_j$ must be linearly independent) is often **assumed silently** in exam problems. If the question states "redundant constraints" or "gradients are dependent", the standard Lagrange method fails.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Structural Design:** Minimize weight of a truss subject to stress equilibrium equations.
- **Process Engineering:** Maximize yield subject to mass-balance (equality) constraints from stoichiometry.
- **Economics:** Utility maximization subject to budget equality constraint.
- **Aerospace:** Minimize drag subject to lift-equals-weight equality at cruise.
- **Machine Learning:** Support Vector Machines use Lagrangian duality with equality margin constraints.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the First-Order Necessary Condition (Lagrange Condition)

We consider the simplest non-trivial case: **Minimize** $f(x, y)$ subject to $g(x, y) = 0$, with $f, g \in \mathcal{C}^1$.

#### Step 1: Parametrize the Constraint Surface

Since $g(x, y) = 0$ defines a smooth curve, by the **Implicit Function Theorem** (valid when $g_y \neq 0$), we can locally solve for $y$ as $y = \phi(x)$:

$$g(x, \phi(x)) = 0$$

#### Step 2: Substitute into the Objective

Define a reduced single-variable function:

$$h(x) \equiv f(x, \phi(x))$$

The unconstrained optimum of $h(x)$ corresponds to the constrained optimum of $f$ on the curve.

#### Step 3: Apply the Standard Calculus First-Order Condition

$$\frac{dh}{dx} = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial f}{\partial y} \cdot \frac{d\phi}{dx} = 0$$

#### Step 4: Obtain $\dfrac{d\phi}{dx}$ from the Implicit Function

Differentiating $g(x, \phi(x)) = 0$ w.r.t. $x$:

$$\frac{\partial g}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial g}{\partial y} \cdot \frac{d\phi}{dx} = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{d\phi}{dx} = -\frac{g_x}{g_y}$$

#### Step 5: Combine to Eliminate $\phi$

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial f}{\partial y} \left( -\frac{g_x}{g_y} \right) = 0$$

Multiplying through by $g_y$:

$$f_x \, g_y - f_y \, g_x = 0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad f_x \, g_y = f_y \, g_x$$

#### Step 6: Recognize the Cross-Product Structure

In vector form, this is the **2D cross product** being zero, which is the condition for **two vectors to be parallel**:

$$\nabla f = (f_x, f_y) \quad \text{and} \quad \nabla g = (g_x, g_y) \quad \text{are parallel}$$

So there exists a scalar $\lambda$ such that:

$$\nabla f = \lambda \, \nabla g$$

which is the **Lagrange Condition** for $m = 1$ constraint.

#### Step 7: Generalize to $m$ Constraints

For $m$ constraints, the $m$ gradient vectors $\nabla g_1, \ldots, \nabla g_m$ span an $m$-dimensional subspace. The Lagrange condition requires that $\nabla f$ lie in this subspace:

$$\nabla f = \sum_{j=1}^{m} \lambda_j \nabla g_j$$

which is the gradient of $L(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}) = f(\mathbf{x}) - \sum_j \lambda_j g_j(\mathbf{x})$ w.r.t. $\mathbf{x}$, set to zero. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Worked Example — KTU Typical Style Problem

> **Problem:** Find the extremum of $f(x, y) = x^2 + y^2$ subject to $x + y = 4$, and classify the optimum.

#### Step 1: Construct the Lagrangian

$$L(x, y, \lambda) = x^2 + y^2 - \lambda(x + y - 4)$$

#### Step 2: Apply Stationarity Conditions

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial x} = 2x - \lambda = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \lambda = 2x$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial y} = 2y - \lambda = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \lambda = 2y$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \lambda} = -(x + y - 4) = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad x + y = 4$$

#### Step 3: Solve the System

From the first two: $2x = 2y \Rightarrow x = y$. Substituting into the constraint: $2x = 4 \Rightarrow x = 2, \, y = 2$. Then $\lambda = 4$.

#### Step 4: Classify Using the Bordered Hessian

The constraint gradient is $\nabla g = (1, 1)$, and the Hessian of $f$ is $\mathbf{H}_f = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$.

The bordered Hessian is:

$$\mathbf{H}_B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

The principal minors (after removing the row/column for $\lambda$):

$$\det\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix} = 4 \quad (\text{last } n-m = 1 \text{ minor})$$

For a **minimum** with $m=1$, the sign must be $(-1)^1 = -$. But our minor is **positive** ($+4$). 

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Resolution:** For the *reduced* test on the tangent subspace, we substitute $y = 4 - x$ into $f$ to get $h(x) = x^2 + (4-x)^2 = 2x^2 - 8x + 16$. Then $h''(x) = 4 > 0$, confirming a **minimum** at $x = 2$. The bordered Hessian sign test for $m=1$ in 2D requires looking at the **determinant of the leading $3 \times 3$ block with row/column pivoting** — for a minimum with $m=1$, the sign of the last $n-m = 1$ principal minor of the *reduced* (constrained) Hessian must be positive. The optimal point is $\mathbf{x}^* = (2, 2)$ with $f^* = 8$, and $\lambda^* = 4$ (interpreted: relaxing the constraint by 1 unit would increase the minimum objective by 4 units).

### 3.3 Multi-Constraint Case — General Algorithm

**Algorithm: Lagrange Multiplier Method**

1. **Form** $L(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}) = f(\mathbf{x}) - \sum_{j=1}^{m} \lambda_j g_j(\mathbf{x})$.
2. **Compute** $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial x_i} = 0$ for $i = 1, \ldots, n$ → yields $n$ equations.
3. **Compute** $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \lambda_j} = 0$ for $j = 1, \ldots, m$ → reproduces the constraints.
4. **Solve** the resulting $(n + m) \times (n + m)$ nonlinear system.
5. **Verify** constraint qualification: $\text{rank}(\nabla g(\mathbf{x}^*)) = m$.
6. **Classify** using the Bordered Hessian sign test.
7. **Report** $\mathbf{x}^*, f(\mathbf{x}^*)$, and $\lambda_j^*$ (with economic interpretation if applicable).

### 3.4 Second-Order Sufficient Condition (Detailed)

For a problem with $m$ equality constraints, define the **reduced bordered Hessian** as the $(n+m-q) \times (n+m-q)$ determinant for $q = 1, 2, \ldots, n-m$:

$$H_q = \det \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{0} & \nabla g^T \\ \nabla g & \mathbf{H}_f \end{bmatrix}_{(n+m-q) \times (n+m-q)}$$

**Sufficient Condition for a Local Minimum:**
$$\text{sign}(H_q) = (-1)^m \quad \text{for all } q = 1, 2, \ldots, n-m$$

**Sufficient Condition for a Local Maximum:**
$$\text{sign}(H_q) = (-1)^{n-q} \quad \text{for all } q = 1, 2, \ldots, n-m$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Simplified Single-Constraint Rule ($m=1$):** The test reduces to checking the sign of the *constrained Hessian* (Hessian of $L$ restricted to the tangent plane of $g=0$). For a **minimum**, the restricted Hessian must be **positive definite**; for a **maximum**, it must be **negative definite**.

### 3.5 Case of Two Equality Constraints ($m=2$)

When $m=2$ and $n$ variables, the bordered Hessian is $(n+2) \times (n+2)$. We examine the last $n-2$ principal minors. For a minimum, all must have sign $(-1)^2 = +$. For a maximum, signs alternate as $(-1)^{n-q}$.

**Special check:** If $n = 2$ and $m = 2$, the system is fully determined and there are no free dimensions — the "optimum" is simply the unique intersection of the two constraint curves (no classification needed).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow of Constrained Optimization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Define Objective f and Equality Constraints gj] --> B[Form Lagrangian L = f minus sum lambda j times gj]
    B --> C[Compute partial derivatives of L with respect to x_i]
    C --> D{Set gradient of L to zero: n equations}
    D --> E[Add m feasibility equations: gj equals zero]
    E --> F[Solve the n plus m nonlinear system]
    F --> G{Check Constraint Qualification: rank of grad g equals m?}
    G -->|No| H[FAIL: Multipliers may not exist - use alternative method]
    G -->|Yes| I[Obtain candidate point x star and lambda star]
    I --> J[Form Bordered Hessian H_B]
    J --> K{Apply Second Order Test: signs of last n minus m principal minors}
    K -->|Min pattern: sign equals -1 to power m| L[CONFIRMED LOCAL MINIMUM]
    K -->|Max pattern: sign equals -1 to power n minus q| M[CONFIRMED LOCAL MAXIMUM]
    K -->|Inconsistent signs| N[SADDLE POINT OR NO EXTREMUM]
    L --> O[REPORT x star, f star, and lambda star with interpretation]
    M --> O
    N --> O
```

### 4.2 Geometric Interpretation — Tangency Condition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Level set of f: f equals constant] --> C{At the optimum}
    B[Constraint surface: g equals zero] --> C
    C --> D[Gradient of f is parallel to Gradient of g]
    D --> E[Lagrange Condition: grad f equals lambda times grad g]
    E --> F[Multiplier lambda represents the rate of change of f with respect to relaxing the constraint]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture for the Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Input: f, vector g, dimension n, number of constraints m] --> P2[Lagrangian Construction Block]
    P2 --> P3[Gradient Computation Block: n plus m partial derivatives]
    P3 --> P4[Nonlinear Solver Block: Newton Raphson or Symbolic Solver]
    P4 --> P5[Constraint Qualification Verifier Block]
    P5 -->|Satisfied| P6[Bordered Hessian Assembler Block]
    P5 -->|Violated| P7[Warning Output Block]
    P6 --> P8[Principal Minor Sign Analyzer Block]
    P8 --> P9[Classification Decision Block: Min / Max / Saddle]
    P9 --> P10[Result Output: x star, f star, lambda star]
    P7 --> P10
```

### 4.4 Visualization of the Tangency Concept (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph "Constraint Surface g(x) = 0"
        CS[Curved Manifold in R^n]
    end
    subgraph "Level Set of Objective f(x) = c"
        LS[Hyper surface Contour]
    end
    subgraph "Optimal Point"
        OP[Tangency: Two surfaces touch without crossing]
    end
    CS --> OP
    LS --> OP
    OP --> V1[grad f points outward]
    OP --> V2[grad g is normal to the surface]
    V1 --> V3[Parallelism: grad f equals lambda times grad g]
    V2 --> V3
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*State the first-order necessary condition for an extremum of a function $f(\mathbf{x})$ subject to the equality constraint $g(\mathbf{x}) = 0$. What is the geometric meaning of the Lagrange multiplier $\lambda$?*

**Model Answer:**

The first-order necessary condition is the **Lagrange condition**: at a local extremum $\mathbf{x}^*$, there exists a scalar $\lambda^*$ such that

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \lambda^* \, \nabla g(\mathbf{x}^*)$$

provided $\nabla g(\mathbf{x}^*) \neq \mathbf{0}$ (constraint qualification). Equivalently, using the Lagrangian $L = f - \lambda g$:

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} L(\mathbf{x}^*, \lambda^*) = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad g(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$$

**Geometric meaning of $\lambda$:** The Lagrange multiplier $\lambda^*$ represents the **rate of change of the optimal objective value $f^*$** with respect to a unit **relaxation** of the constraint. If we replace $g(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ with $g(\mathbf{x}) = \epsilon$ (a small constant), then to first order:

$$f^*(\epsilon) \approx f^*(0) + \lambda^* \cdot \epsilon$$

Hence $\lambda^* = \dfrac{df^*}{d\epsilon}\bigg|_{\epsilon=0}$ is the **sensitivity** (or "shadow price") of the optimal value to constraint perturbation.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Question]**
*What is a "constraint qualification"? Why is it needed in the Lagrange multiplier method?*

**Model Answer:**

A **constraint qualification (CQ)** is a regularity condition on the constraint functions at the candidate optimum $\mathbf{x}^*$. The most common CQ is the **linear independence constraint qualification (LICQ)**: the gradients $\nabla g_1(\mathbf{x}^*), \ldots, \nabla g_m(\mathbf{x}^*)$ must be **linearly independent**, i.e.,

$$\text{rank}\big[\nabla g_1(\mathbf{x}^*) \;\; \nabla g_2(\mathbf{x}^*) \;\; \cdots \;\; \nabla g_m(\mathbf{x}^*)\big] = m$$

**Why it is needed:** The standard Lagrange multiplier theorem guarantees the *existence* of multipliers $\lambda_1^*, \ldots, \lambda_m^*$ **only** when a CQ holds. Without it:
- The multipliers may not exist even though a constrained optimum does.
- The system $\nabla L = 0$ may have spurious solutions.
- The Bordered Hessian test becomes ill-conditioned.

In KTU exam problems, CQ is usually assumed to be satisfied; if a problem mentions "degenerate" or "redundant" constraints, the student must check LICQ explicitly.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### ⭐ CHOICE A (14 Marks)

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Find the extremum of $f(x, y, z) = x^2 + y^2 + z^2$ subject to the constraint $x + y + z = 9$, and classify it using the second-order test.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A rectangular box is to be designed with a fixed surface area of $S = 12 \, \text{m}^2$. Find the dimensions that **minimize the volume** using the Lagrange multiplier method. State the minimum volume.

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Step 1: Form the Lagrangian**

$$L(x, y, z, \lambda) = x^2 + y^2 + z^2 - \lambda(x + y + z - 9)$$

**Step 2: Stationarity conditions**

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial x} = 2x - \lambda = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \lambda = 2x$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial y} = 2y - \lambda = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \lambda = 2y$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial z} = 2z - \lambda = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \lambda = 2z$$

**Step 3: Feasibility**

$$x + y + z = 9$$

**Step 4: Solve**

From the first three: $2x = 2y = 2z \Rightarrow x = y = z$. Substituting: $3x = 9 \Rightarrow x = 3, \, y = 3, \, z = 3$. Then $\lambda = 6$.

**Step 5: Bordered Hessian classification**

$\mathbf{H}_f = 2\mathbf{I}_3$ and $\nabla g = (1, 1, 1)$. The bordered Hessian is:

$$\mathbf{H}_B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 2 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

The reduced Hessian (substituting $z = 9 - x - y$ into $f$ gives $h(x,y) = x^2 + y^2 + (9-x-y)^2$):

$$\nabla^2 h = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 2 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$$

Eigenvalues are $6$ and $2$ — both **positive**, so the restricted Hessian is **positive definite** → **Local Minimum**.

**Result:** $\mathbf{x}^* = (3, 3, 3)$, $f^* = 27$, $\lambda^* = 6$. (The function $f$ measures squared distance from the origin; this is the point on the plane $x+y+z=9$ closest to the origin.)

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Step 1: Formulate the problem**

Let the box have dimensions $x, y, z > 0$.

- **Objective:** Minimize Volume $V = xyz$.
- **Constraint:** Surface area $S = 2(xy + yz + zx) = 12$, i.e., $g(x,y,z) = xy + yz + zx - 6 = 0$.

**Step 2: Form the Lagrangian**

$$L(x, y, z, \lambda) = xyz - \lambda(xy + yz + zx - 6)$$

**Step 3: Stationarity conditions**

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial x} = yz - \lambda(y + z) = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial y} = xz - \lambda(x + z) = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial z} = xy - \lambda(x + y) = 0$$

**Step 4: Solve (by symmetry)**

By symmetry of the problem, the optimum must satisfy $x = y = z$. Let $x = y = z = a$.

- Constraint: $3a^2 = 6 \Rightarrow a = \sqrt{2}$ m.
- Lagrangian equations: $a^2 - \lambda(2a) = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = a/2 = \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$.

**Step 5: Minimum Volume**

$$V^* = (\sqrt{2})^3 = 2\sqrt{2} \approx 2.828 \; \text{m}^3$$

**Step 6: Confirm minimum via second-order test (log-concavity of $\ln V$ subject to linearized constraint, or bordered Hessian).**

The bordered Hessian at the critical point is a $4 \times 4$ matrix. By the symmetry and strict convexity of the feasible set intersected with $V$, this is a global minimum on the positive orthant.

**Result:** $x = y = z = \sqrt{2}$ m, $V_{\min} = 2\sqrt{2}$ m³, $\lambda^* = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$.

---

#### ⭐ CHOICE B (14 Marks)

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Use the Lagrange multiplier method to find the **maximum** of $f(x, y) = xy$ subject to $x^2 + y^2 = 1$. Classify the result.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Minimize $f(x, y, z) = 4x - 2y + 4z$ subject to $2x^2 + y^2 + z^2 = 9$. Comment on the role of the Lagrange multiplier in this problem.

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Step 1: Form the Lagrangian**

$$L(x, y, \lambda) = xy - \lambda(x^2 + y^2 - 1)$$

**Step 2: Stationarity conditions**

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial x} = y - 2\lambda x = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad y = 2\lambda x$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial y} = x - 2\lambda y = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad x = 2\lambda y$$

**Step 3: Solve**

From the first: $y = 2\lambda x$. Substituting into the second:

$$x = 2\lambda(2\lambda x) = 4\lambda^2 x$$

If $x \neq 0$: $\lambda^2 = \dfrac{1}{4} \Rightarrow \lambda = \pm \dfrac{1}{2}$.

**Case 1: $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{2}$:** $y = x$. Constraint: $2x^2 = 1 \Rightarrow x = \pm \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$.

- $(x, y) = \left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$: $f = \dfrac{1}{2}$ (Maximum candidate).
- $(x, y) = \left(-\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, -\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$: $f = \dfrac{1}{2}$ (Maximum candidate).

**Case 2: $\lambda = -\dfrac{1}{2}$:** $y = -x$. Constraint: $2x^2 = 1 \Rightarrow x = \pm \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$.

- $(x, y) = \left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, -\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$: $f = -\dfrac{1}{2}$ (Minimum candidate).
- $(x, y) = \left(-\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$: $f = -\dfrac{1}{2}$ (Minimum candidate).

**Step 4: Bordered Hessian classification**

$\mathbf{H}_f = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$, $\nabla g = (2x, 2y)$.

At $\left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$: $\nabla g = (\sqrt{2}, \sqrt{2})$. The restricted Hessian (with $y = x$ substituted, $f = x^2$, $f'' = 2 > 0$ — wait, this is for min, not max. Let us re-check: substituting $y = 1/x$ from the constraint... Actually, the cleaner way: substitute $y = \sqrt{1-x^2}$, get $f(x) = x\sqrt{1-x^2}$, $f'(x) = \sqrt{1-x^2} - \dfrac{x^2}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} = \dfrac{1-2x^2}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} = 0 \Rightarrow x = 1/\sqrt{2}$, and $f''(1/\sqrt{2}) < 0$ — confirming a **maximum**.

**Result:** $f_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{2}$ at $\left(\pm \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \pm \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$ (same-sign pairs); $f_{\min} = -\dfrac{1}{2}$ at the opposite-sign pairs.

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Step 1: Form the Lagrangian**

$$L(x, y, z, \lambda) = 4x - 2y + 4z - \lambda(2x^2 + y^2 + z^2 - 9)$$

**Step 2: Stationarity conditions**

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial x} = 4 - 4\lambda x = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad x = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial y} = -2 - 2\lambda y = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad y = -\frac{1}{\lambda}$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial z} = 4 - 2\lambda z = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad z = \frac{2}{\lambda}$$

**Step 3: Feasibility**

$$2x^2 + y^2 + z^2 = 9$$

Substituting:

$$2 \cdot \frac{1}{\lambda^2} + \frac{1}{\lambda^2} + \frac{4}{\lambda^2} = \frac{7}{\lambda^2} = 9 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \lambda^2 = \frac{7}{9} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \lambda = \pm \frac{\sqrt{7}}{3}$$

**Step 4: Evaluate at the two candidates**

- For $\lambda = +\dfrac{\sqrt{7}}{3}$: $(x, y, z) = \left(\dfrac{3}{\sqrt{7}}, -\dfrac{3}{\sqrt{7}}, \dfrac{6}{\sqrt{7}}\right)$. Then $f = \dfrac{12}{\sqrt{7}} + \dfrac{6}{\sqrt{7}} + \dfrac{24}{\sqrt{7}} = \dfrac{42}{\sqrt{7}} = 6\sqrt{7}$.

- For $\lambda = -\dfrac{\sqrt{7}}{3}$: $(x, y, z) = \left(-\dfrac{3}{\sqrt{7}}, \dfrac{3}{\sqrt{7}}, -\dfrac{6}{\sqrt{7}}\right)$. Then $f = -\dfrac{12}{\sqrt{7}} - \dfrac{6}{\sqrt{7}} - \dfrac{24}{\sqrt{7}} = -6\sqrt{7}$.

**Step 5: Classify using the Hessian of $L$**

$$\mathbf{H}_L = \text{diag}(-4\lambda, -2\lambda, -2\lambda)$$

- For $\lambda = +\sqrt{7}/3 > 0$: $\mathbf{H}_L = \text{diag}(-\text{positive}, -\text{positive}, -\text{positive})$ — **negative definite** → Maximum.
- For $\lambda = -\sqrt{7}/3 < 0$: $\mathbf{H}_L$ is **positive definite** → Minimum.

**Step 6: Role of the Lagrange multiplier**

Since $f$ is linear, the objective contours are parallel hyperplanes. The constraint $2x^2 + y^2 + z^2 = 9$ is an ellipsoid. The Lagrange multiplier $\lambda$ at the optimum equals the **ratio of the gradient of $f$ projected onto the constraint normal** — geometrically, it represents the "exchange rate" between the ellipsoid's boundary slope and the plane's slope. The negative-sign choice in our Lagrangian would give the *minimum*; we found the maximum with the **positive** $\lambda$ via the sign convention we adopted.

**Result:**
$$f_{\max} = 6\sqrt{7} \approx 15.87 \; \text{ at } \; \left(\tfrac{3}{\sqrt{7}}, -\tfrac{3}{\sqrt{7}}, \tfrac{6}{\sqrt{7}}\right)$$
$$f_{\min} = -6\sqrt{7} \; \text{ at the antipodal point.}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Lagrangian:** Choosing $L = f + \lambda g$ vs. $L = f - \lambda g$ reverses the sign of $\lambda$. This is *acceptable* if done consistently — but mixing signs in the same paper loses marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the Feasibility Equation:** Students often set $\partial L / \partial x_i = 0$ but forget $\partial L / \partial \lambda = 0$ (i.e., the constraint $g = 0$). This leads to a **critical point of the unconstrained problem**, not the constrained one — a **3-mark penalty** typical in KTU valuation.
> 3. **Wrong Sign in the Bordered Hessian Test:** For minimum with $m=1$, the sign of the relevant minor is $(-1)^1 = -$, **NOT** $+$. The most common sign error. Double-check by substituting back.
> 4. **Constraint Qualification Assumption:** If a problem has a *single* equality constraint, CQ (LICQ) means $\nabla g \neq \mathbf{0}$. For $\nabla g = \mathbf{0}$, the constraint is degenerate and the method may fail.
> 5. **Mixing Up the Multi-Constraint Test:** For $m \geq 2$, the test involves the *last* $(n-m)$ principal minors, not the *first* — many students mistakenly use the leading principal minors.
> 6. **Not Verifying the Solution is on the Constraint:** Plug the candidate $(x^*, y^*, z^*)$ back into $g = 0$ before reporting. (Algebraic errors are common in 3-variable systems.)

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final-Five-Minute Revision Box — Master These Before Entering the Exam Hall:**

- [x] **Lagrangian Form:** $L(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}) = f(\mathbf{x}) - \sum_{j=1}^{m} \lambda_j g_j(\mathbf{x})$
- [x] **System Size:** Solve $(n + m)$ equations in $(n + m)$ unknowns $(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda})$.
- [x] **First-Order Condition:** $\nabla f = \sum_{j=1}^{m} \lambda_j \nabla g_j$ at the optimum.
- [x] **Geometric Meaning:** $\nabla f$ and the constraint surface's normal are **parallel** at the optimum.
- [x] **Shadow Price / Sensitivity:** $\lambda_j^* = \dfrac{\partial f^*}{\partial b_j}$ when constraint becomes $g_j = b_j$.
- [x] **Bordered Hessian:** $H_B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & \nabla g^T \\ \nabla g & \mathbf{H}_f \end{bmatrix}$
- [x] **Min Test ($m$ constraints):** Last $n - m$ principal minors have sign $(-1)^m$.
- [x] **Max Test ($m$ constraints):** Last $n - m$ principal minors have sign $(-1)^{n-q}$ for $q = 1, \ldots, n-m$.
- [x] **Constraint Qualification (LICQ):** $\nabla g_1, \ldots, \nabla g_m$ must be linearly independent at $\mathbf{x}^*$.
- [x] **Single-Constraint Shortcut:** Substitute the constraint to reduce to $n - 1$ variables and use the unconstrained second-derivative test.
- [x] **Symmetry Trick:** For problems with symmetric $f$ and constraints (e.g., $x^2+y^2$, $x+y+z$), guess $x = y = z$ first.
- [x] **Economic Interpretation:** $\lambda_j > 0$ for a *binding* constraint at the optimum; if relaxing the constraint helps, then the constraint is "tight."
- [x] **What Bordered Hessian Tells Us:**
  - Positive-definite restricted Hessian $\Rightarrow$ Min
  - Negative-definite restricted Hessian $\Rightarrow$ Max
  - Indefinite $\Rightarrow$ Saddle

**Common KTU 2024 Exam Traps:**
- Always verify **CQ** if the problem has more than one equality constraint.
- Watch for **non-binding** constraints (gradients linearly dependent — discard or combine them).
- A **saddle point** can occur only when $m \geq 2$ in equality-constrained problems; for $m = 1$, the critical point is always an extremum (under CQ).

---

*End of KTU 2024 Scheme Notes — Module 3: Optimization of Function of Multiple Variables Subject to Equality Constraints*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lagrangian Function - Inequality Constrained Techniques - KKT Conditions - Constrained Steepest Descent Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT723 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Lagrangian Function - Inequality Constrained Techniques - KKT Conditions - Constrained Steepest Descent Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Lagrangian Function

The **Lagrangian Function** is a scalar auxiliary function that consolidates the objective function and all constraints (both equality and inequality) of a constrained optimization problem into a single expression. It serves as the mathematical bridge that allows classical unconstrained calculus techniques (differentiation, gradient) to be applied to constrained problems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)**
>
> For a general Nonlinear Programming (NLP) problem:
> $$\min f(\mathbf{x})$$
> $$\text{subject to } g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, m$$
> $$h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \dots, p$$
>
> The **Lagrangian Function** is defined as:
> $$L(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}, \boldsymbol{\mu}) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i g_i(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \mu_j h_j(\mathbf{x})$$
>
> where $\boldsymbol{\lambda} = (\lambda_1, \dots, \lambda_m)$ are the **inequality Lagrange multipliers** and $\boldsymbol{\mu} = (\mu_1, \dots, \mu_p)$ are the **equality Lagrange multipliers**.

## 1.2 The Multipliers — Their Physical Meaning

- **$\lambda_i$ (Inequality Multiplier):** Represents the **shadow price** or marginal worth of relaxing the $i^{th}$ inequality constraint. In engineering economics, it indicates how much the objective improves per unit relaxation.
- **$\mu_j$ (Equality Multiplier):** Represents the force required to maintain the $j^{th}$ equality constraint.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hiking with a Backpack" Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy: Climbing a Mountain with a Heavy Backpack**
>
> Imagine you want to reach the **highest peak** (objective: maximize altitude) in a national park, but you must:
> - **Stay inside the park boundary** (inequality constraint: $g(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$).
> - **Walk only on marked trails** (equality constraint: $h(\mathbf{x}) = 0$).
>
> - The **gradient of $f(\mathbf{x})$** is the steepest uphill direction.
> - The **multiplier $\lambda_i$** acts like the *force exerted by an invisible elastic rope* tied to the park boundary. As long as you are far inside, the rope is slack ($\lambda_i = 0$). The moment you push against the boundary, the rope becomes taut ($\lambda_i > 0$) and pulls you back.
> - The **Lagrangian** combines the mountain's slope and the rope's tension into a single number. Optimizing it means finding the point where the pull of gravity (the gradient) is exactly balanced by the rope tension (the constraint force).

## 1.4 Inequality Constrained Techniques — Overview

Inequality constraints $g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$ partition the design space into **feasible** and **infeasible** regions. A constraint is said to be:

- **Active** at $\mathbf{x}^*$ if $g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$ (the solution lies on the boundary).
- **Inactive** at $\mathbf{x}^*$ if $g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) < 0$ (the solution is strictly inside the feasible region).

## 1.5 KKT Conditions — The Crown Jewel of Constrained Optimization

The **Karush–Kuhn–Tucker (KKT) conditions** are a set of *first-order necessary conditions* for a local optimum of a nonlinear program with inequality and equality constraints. They generalize the classical method of Lagrange multipliers to include inequality constraints. Under suitable **regularity (constraint qualification)** assumptions, any local optimum **must** satisfy the KKT conditions.

## 1.6 Constrained Steepest Descent Method — The Big Picture

The **Constrained Steepest Descent Method** is an iterative search algorithm that moves from a feasible starting point towards the optimum by following the **negative gradient of the Lagrangian** projected onto the feasible region (tangent space of active constraints). It is the constrained analogue of the classical Cauchy steepest descent.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 General Constrained NLP — Canonical Form

For the rest of this module, we adopt the KTU 2024 standard formulation:

$$\min f(\mathbf{x}) \quad \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$$

$$g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, m$$

$$h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \dots, p$$

where $f, g_i, h_j$ are continuously differentiable functions, and $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)^T$.

## 2.2 The Lagrangian Function — Component Analysis

The Lagrangian has three distinct components:

1. **Objective term:** $f(\mathbf{x})$ — drives the optimization towards the optimum.
2. **Inequality penalty terms:** $\lambda_i g_i(\mathbf{x})$ — penalize violation of the upper bounds.
3. **Equality penalty terms:** $\mu_j h_j(\mathbf{x})$ — penalize deviation from exact equality.

**Why does it work?** Because if a constraint is satisfied, the multiplier vanishes (for inactive inequalities) or contributes a known force, allowing the function to behave like the unconstrained objective.

## 2.3 KKT Conditions — Statement

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — KKT Necessary Conditions**
>
> Let $\mathbf{x}^*$ be a local minimum of the NLP and assume a regularity condition (e.g., **LICQ — Linear Independence Constraint Qualification**) holds. Then there exist multipliers $\lambda_i^* \ge 0$ and $\mu_j^*$ such that:
>
> **① Stationarity (Gradient Condition):**
> $$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i^* \nabla g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \mu_j^* \nabla h_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$$
>
> **② Primal Feasibility:**
> $$g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) \le 0, \quad h_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$$
>
> **③ Dual Feasibility:**
> $$\lambda_i^* \ge 0, \quad i = 1, \dots, m$$
>
> **④ Complementary Slackness:**
> $$\lambda_i^* \, g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0, \quad i = 1, \dots, m$$

## 2.4 Interpretation of Complementary Slackness

This condition states that for each inequality constraint, **at least one** of the following must be zero:
- The constraint is active ($g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$), OR
- The corresponding multiplier is zero ($\lambda_i^* = 0$).

If a constraint is **strictly inactive** (slack), its multiplier is zero — meaning the constraint exerts no influence on the optimum. This is the most important KKT concept tested in KTU exams.

## 2.5 KKT Sufficient Conditions

If $f(\mathbf{x})$ is **convex**, $g_i(\mathbf{x})$ are **convex functions**, $h_j(\mathbf{x})$ are **affine (linear)**, and the KKT conditions are satisfied at a feasible point $\mathbf{x}^*$, then $\mathbf{x}^*$ is the **global minimum**.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Mathematical Expression | Condition / Note |
|---|---------|------------------------|------------------|
| 1 | Lagrangian | $L(\mathbf{x},\boldsymbol{\lambda},\boldsymbol{\mu}) = f + \sum \lambda_i g_i + \sum \mu_j h_j$ | Auxiliary scalar function |
| 2 | Stationarity | $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} L = \mathbf{0}$ | Gradient vanishes |
| 3 | Primal Feasibility | $g_i \le 0, \quad h_j = 0$ | Original constraints |
| 4 | Dual Feasibility | $\lambda_i \ge 0$ | Inequality sign |
| 5 | Complementary Slackness | $\lambda_i g_i = 0$ | Most distinctive KKT rule |
| 6 | Lagrangian Gradient | $\nabla f + \sum \lambda_i \nabla g_i + \sum \mu_j \nabla h_j = \mathbf{0}$ | Vector equation |
| 7 | Active Set | $\mathcal{A}(\mathbf{x}) = \{i : g_i(\mathbf{x}) = 0\}$ | Index of active constraints |
| 8 | Steepest Descent Direction (Unconstrained) | $\mathbf{d}_k = -\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ | Negated gradient |
| 9 | Projected Steepest Descent Direction | $\mathbf{d}_k = -P \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ | $P$ = projection matrix |
| 10 | Armijo Step Rule | $f(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha_k \mathbf{d}_k) \le f(\mathbf{x}_k) + c_1 \alpha_k \nabla f^T \mathbf{d}_k$ | $0 < c_1 < 1$ |
| 11 | Kuhn-Tucker Theorem | KKT conditions $\Rightarrow$ local optimum (under regularity) | Necessary |
| 12 | Fritz John Conditions | KKT without $\lambda_0 = 1$ | Weaker form |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility**
> - **Structural Optimization:** Minimize weight subject to stress constraints — multipliers give the marginal cost of relaxing a stress bound.
> - **Power Systems:** Economic dispatch minimizes fuel cost subject to power balance (equality) and generator limits (inequality).
> - **Process Engineering:** Reactor design optimizes yield subject to temperature and pressure limits.
> - **Machine Learning (SVMs):** KKT conditions determine which training samples become **support vectors**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Lagrange Multipliers to KKT

### Step 1: Start with Equality-Constrained Problem

For $\min f(\mathbf{x})$ subject to $h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$, the classical method requires:

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = -\sum_{j=1}^{p} \mu_j \nabla h_j(\mathbf{x}^*)$$

This is derived by introducing the Lagrangian and setting $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} L = 0$.

### Step 2: Add an Inequality Constraint

Now consider an additional constraint $g(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$. Two cases arise:

**Case A:** At the optimum $\mathbf{x}^*$, the inequality is **inactive** ($g(\mathbf{x}^*) < 0$). Then it can be ignored and the solution reduces to the equality case.

**Case B:** At the optimum $\mathbf{x}^*$, the inequality is **active** ($g(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$). Then it behaves locally like an equality constraint.

### Step 3: Sign of the Multiplier

In a minimization problem, increasing $g(\mathbf{x})$ (moving into infeasible region) should **decrease** the Lagrangian (since the objective is being worsened and the constraint is being violated). This forces:

$$\lambda \ge 0 \quad \text{(Dual Feasibility)}$$

### Step 4: Combine into a Single System

Stationarity:
$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) + \lambda \nabla g(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$$

Complementary slackness:
$$\lambda g(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$$

This compact system is the essence of the KKT conditions.

## 3.2 Comprehensive Worked Example — KKT Application

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU Typical 14-Mark Pattern)**
>
> $$\min f(x_1, x_2) = (x_1 - 1)^2 + (x_2 - 2)^2$$
> $$\text{subject to } g_1(\mathbf{x}) = x_1 + x_2 - 3 \le 0$$
> $$g_2(\mathbf{x}) = -x_1 \le 0$$
> $$g_3(\mathbf{x}) = -x_2 \le 0$$

**Step 1: Form the Lagrangian**

$$L(x_1, x_2, \lambda_1, \lambda_2, \lambda_3) = (x_1-1)^2 + (x_2-2)^2 + \lambda_1(x_1 + x_2 - 3) + \lambda_2(-x_1) + \lambda_3(-x_2)$$

**Step 2: Compute the Stationarity Conditions**

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial x_1} = 2(x_1 - 1) + \lambda_1 - \lambda_2 = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial x_2} = 2(x_2 - 2) + \lambda_1 - \lambda_3 = 0$$

**Step 3: Hypothesize the Active Set**

The unconstrained minimum is at $(1, 2)$. Check feasibility: $1 + 2 - 3 = 0$. Thus $g_1$ is **active**. $g_2(1,2) = -1 < 0$ (inactive), $g_3(1,2) = -2 < 0$ (inactive).

**Step 4: Apply Complementary Slackness**

Since $g_2$ and $g_3$ are inactive, $\lambda_2 = 0$ and $\lambda_3 = 0$.

Stationarity reduces to:
$$2(x_1 - 1) + \lambda_1 = 0 \implies x_1 = 1 - \frac{\lambda_1}{2}$$

$$2(x_2 - 2) + \lambda_1 = 0 \implies x_2 = 2 - \frac{\lambda_1}{2}$$

**Step 5: Enforce Active Constraint**

$$g_1(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0 \implies x_1 + x_2 - 3 = 0$$

$$\left(1 - \frac{\lambda_1}{2}\right) + \left(2 - \frac{\lambda_1}{2}\right) - 3 = 0$$

$$3 - \lambda_1 - 3 = 0 \implies \lambda_1 = 0$$

**Step 6: Solve and Verify**

With $\lambda_1 = 0$: $x_1 = 1$, $x_2 = 2$. Check dual feasibility: $\lambda_1 = 0 \ge 0$ ✓

**Step 7: Final Answer**

$$\boxed{\mathbf{x}^* = (1, 2)^T, \quad f(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0, \quad \lambda_1 = 0}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Since $\lambda_1 = 0$, the constraint $x_1 + x_2 \le 3$ is **not binding** the optimum. Removing it would not change the solution. This is the practical power of the KKT multiplier as a sensitivity indicator.

## 3.3 Worked Example — Case with Active Multiplier

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem**
>
> $$\min f(x_1, x_2) = x_1^2 + x_2^2$$
> $$\text{subject to } g_1: x_1 + x_2 - 2 \le 0$$
> $$g_2: -x_1 \le 0, \quad g_3: -x_2 \le 0$$

**Step 1: Lagrangian**

$$L = x_1^2 + x_2^2 + \lambda_1(x_1 + x_2 - 2) - \lambda_2 x_1 - \lambda_3 x_2$$

**Step 2: Stationarity**

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial x_1} = 2x_1 + \lambda_1 - \lambda_2 = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial x_2} = 2x_2 + \lambda_1 - \lambda_3 = 0$$

**Step 3: Assume $g_1$ is Active, Others Inactive**

Then $\lambda_2 = \lambda_3 = 0$:
$$2x_1 + \lambda_1 = 0 \implies x_1 = -\frac{\lambda_1}{2}$$
$$2x_2 + \lambda_1 = 0 \implies x_2 = -\frac{\lambda_1}{2}$$

**Step 4: Substitute into Active Constraint**

$$x_1 + x_2 - 2 = 0 \implies -\lambda_1 = 2 \implies \lambda_1 = -2$$

**Step 5: Check Dual Feasibility**

We require $\lambda_1 \ge 0$, but we got $\lambda_1 = -2 < 0$. **Hypothesis violated.**

**Step 6: Re-evaluate — Inactive Constraint**

If $g_1$ is inactive, then $\lambda_1 = 0$ and the problem reduces to unconstrained minimization:
$$2x_1 = 0 \implies x_1 = 0, \quad 2x_2 = 0 \implies x_2 = 0$$

Check feasibility: $0 + 0 - 2 = -2 \le 0$ ✓, $-0 \le 0$ ✓, $-0 \le 0$ ✓.

**Step 7: Final Answer**

$$\boxed{\mathbf{x}^* = (0, 0)^T, \quad f^* = 0, \quad \lambda_1 = \lambda_2 = \lambda_3 = 0}$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Constrained Steepest Descent Method

### 3.4.1 Algorithm Concept

Starting from a feasible $\mathbf{x}_0$, the algorithm iteratively updates:

$$\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k + \alpha_k \mathbf{d}_k$$

where $\mathbf{d}_k$ is the feasible descent direction and $\alpha_k$ is the step length.

### 3.4.2 Direction Computation

For an **inequality-constrained** problem with active set $\mathcal{A}_k$ at iteration $k$, the steepest feasible descent direction is found by solving:

$$\min \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^T \mathbf{d} + \frac{1}{2} \mathbf{d}^T \mathbf{d}$$

$$\text{subject to } \nabla g_i(\mathbf{x}_k)^T \mathbf{d} \le 0 \quad \forall i \in \mathcal{A}_k$$

This is a quadratic subproblem whose **KKT conditions** give the direction:

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) + \sum_{i \in \mathcal{A}_k} \lambda_i \nabla g_i(\mathbf{x}_k) + \mathbf{d} = \mathbf{0}$$

$$\mathbf{d} = -\left[\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) + \sum_{i \in \mathcal{A}_k} \lambda_i \nabla g_i(\mathbf{x}_k)\right]$$

### 3.4.3 Step Length — Armijo Rule

Choose the largest $\alpha_k = \beta \alpha_{k-1}$ (with $\beta \in (0,1)$) such that:

$$f(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha_k \mathbf{d}_k) \le f(\mathbf{x}_k) + c_1 \alpha_k \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^T \mathbf{d}_k$$

with $c_1 \in (0, 1)$, typically $c_1 = 10^{-4}$.

### 3.4.4 Active Set Update

After each iteration, the active set is revised:
- Add a constraint that becomes binding.
- Remove a constraint whose multiplier becomes zero.

This is the **Gradient Projection Method of Rosen** (1960).

## 3.5 Summary of Analytical Steps

| Step | Operation | Output |
|------|-----------|--------|
| 1 | Formulate Lagrangian | $L(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}, \boldsymbol{\mu})$ |
| 2 | Compute $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} L = 0$ | Stationarity equations |
| 3 | Apply complementary slackness | Reduces active set |
| 4 | Enforce primal feasibility | Validates solution |
| 5 | Verify dual feasibility ($\lambda \ge 0$) | Confirms optimum type |
| 6 | For steepest descent: project gradient | Feasible direction |
| 7 | Perform line search | Step length $\alpha_k$ |
| 8 | Update active set | Next iteration |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — KKT Conditions Solution Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: NLP Problem"] --> B["Form Lagrangian L"]
    B --> C["Compute Grad x L equals 0"]
    C --> D["Identify Candidate Active Set"]
    D --> E["Apply Complementary Slackness"]
    E --> F["Solve Stationarity + Active Constraints"]
    F --> G{"Dual Feasibility lambda ge 0?"}
    G -- "Yes" --> H["Check Primal Feasibility gi le 0"]
    G -- "No" --> D
    H --> I{"All Constraints Satisfied?"}
    I -- "Yes" --> J["Solution is KKT Point"]
    I -- "No" --> D
    J --> K["Verify Sufficiency Convexity Check"]
    K --> L["Declare Optimal Solution x star"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Constrained Steepest Descent Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["Initialize x0 feasible"] --> S1["Set k equals 0"]
    S1 --> S2["Determine Active Set A_k at x_k"]
    S2 --> S3["Compute Projected Gradient d_k"]
    S3 --> S4{"Is d_k equal to zero?"}
    S4 -- "Yes" --> S5["Compute Lagrange Multipliers"]
    S5 --> S6{"All Multipliers ge 0?"}
    S6 -- "Yes" --> S7["STOP KKT Point Found"]
    S6 -- "No" --> S8["Drop Constraint with Negative Multiplier"]
    S8 --> S3
    S4 -- "No" --> S9["Line Search for Step alpha_k"]
    S9 --> S10["Update x_{k+1} = x_k + alpha_k d_k"]
    S10 --> S11["k equals k+1"]
    S11 --> S2
```

block-beta

        A["f(x) Objective"]
    end
